docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3413:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3269:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:1868:docs/decisions/0008-twitter-clone-on-ios-as-the-slice-target.md:94:**Exit gate.** Cold launch with primed LMDB renders the seed-driven timeline in ≤ 1.5s, showing fresh content from hundreds of authors followed by the seeds; tap an author → profile screen → back works; kind:0 arriving mid-scroll updates all author rows in place per doctrine D1; updating one seed's kind:3 mid-session re-resolves the timeline's author set without manual intervention; reactivity-bench `--standard` continues to pass at the larger author-set size; firehose-bench `live` for `sustained_firehose` (running at the real seed-author scale, not modeled) lands within budgets.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3415:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3271:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:1874:docs/design/podcast/podcast-core.md:240:17 `ViewModule`s. Each in `podcast-core/src/views/<name>.rs`. All share `View*` payload conventions: pre-formatted strings (per doctrine D1), no business logic in Swift, composite-keyed `ViewDependencies` (per ADR-0001).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3416:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3272:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:1889:docs/decisions/0006-vertical-slice-first.md:112:- **Subsequent expansion has a working substrate to build on.** Adding LMDB is a `Box<dyn EventStore>` swap. Adding multi-relay is a planner change. Adding negentropy is a planner change. Adding iOS is a UniFFI wrap of the existing actor. None require redesigning the architecture.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3418:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3274:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:1901:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:68:+**`nostr-lmdb` gives us** (per `docs.rs/nostr-lmdb`): `save_event(&Event)`, `event_by_id(&EventId)`, `check_id(&EventId) -> DatabaseEventStatus`, `query(Filter) -> Events`, `count(Filter)`, `delete(Filter)`, `wipe()`, `negentropy_items(Filter) -> Vec<(EventId, Timestamp)>`. It owns the page allocator, the LMDB environment, primary by-id store, indexes derived from `Filter`, replaceable / parameterized-replaceable supersession, and NIP-09 delete handling. It is the only mature Rust LMDB store for Nostr events with proven NIP-77 integration; reinventing it is a year of work that we will not recoup.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3437:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3293:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:1975:docs/perf/codex-reviews/0dfb975.md:1441:+17 `ViewModule`s. Each in `podcast-core/src/views/<name>.rs`. All share `View*` payload conventions: pre-formatted strings (per doctrine D1), no business logic in Swift, composite-keyed `ViewDependencies` (per ADR-0001).

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3510:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3366:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2070:docs/perf/codex-reviews/2026-05-18-session-1.md:576:+> **The doctrine is final** (`docs/product-spec.md` §1.5): D0 kernel never grows app nouns · D1 best-effort rendering with placeholders · D2 reactivity contract (composite reverse index, ≤60Hz/view, working-set bound) · D3 errors never cross FFI · D4 one writer per fact · D5 capabilities report, never decide. Every PR is reviewed against this rubric; a change that makes any doctrine harder to enforce is rewritten or rejected.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3511:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3367:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2071:docs/perf/codex-reviews/2026-05-18-session-1.md:601:+**Why this milestone exists separately.** Every milestone M1–M10 has run iOS measurements, but each in service of its own feature. M10.5 is the dedicated *FFI surface* hardening pass — finding and fixing every shape of FFI bug that a non-social-domain consumer (M11 podcast app) would otherwise discover the hard way. This is also the milestone where we delete every shortcut and "TODO: revisit" comment in the FFI layer.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3598:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3454:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2196:docs/design/ffi-hardening/gates.md:241:- 📝 **Sign-off:** doctrine-review.md § D5.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3605:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3461:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2211:docs/design/lmdb-schema.md:17:**`nostr-lmdb` gives us** (per `docs.rs/nostr-lmdb`): `save_event(&Event)`, `event_by_id(&EventId)`, `check_id(&EventId) -> DatabaseEventStatus`, `query(Filter) -> Events`, `count(Filter)`, `delete(Filter)`, `wipe()`, `negentropy_items(Filter) -> Vec<(EventId, Timestamp)>`. It owns the page allocator, the LMDB environment, primary by-id store, indexes derived from `Filter`, replaceable / parameterized-replaceable supersession, and NIP-09 delete handling. It is the only mature Rust LMDB store for Nostr events with proven NIP-77 integration; reinventing it is a year of work that we will not recoup.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3640:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3496:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2690:    20	- **Live Nostr-connected iOS app** in `ios/NmpStress` (~1,375 LOC Swift): SwiftUI shell wired to the Rust kernel via raw C FFI. Connects to `wss://relay.primal.net` (content) + `wss://purplepag.es` (indexer). Renders seed-driven timeline from union of pablof7z + fiatjaf + jb55 follow lists. Profile resolution with placeholders → in-place refinement on kind:0 arrival per doctrine D1. Thread view. Diagnostics screen showing relay status, logical interests, wire subscriptions (ADR-0007).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3648:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3504:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2736:    66	5. **The kernel never grows app nouns.** ADR-0009 doctrine D0 is enforced by review and by the M11 podcast-app proof.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3656:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3512:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2866:    17	**`nostr-lmdb` gives us** (per `docs.rs/nostr-lmdb`): `save_event(&Event)`, `event_by_id(&EventId)`, `check_id(&EventId) -> DatabaseEventStatus`, `query(Filter) -> Events`, `count(Filter)`, `delete(Filter)`, `wipe()`, `negentropy_items(Filter) -> Vec<(EventId, Timestamp)>`. It owns the page allocator, the LMDB environment, primary by-id store, indexes derived from `Filter`, replaceable / parameterized-replaceable supersession, and NIP-09 delete handling. It is the only mature Rust LMDB store for Nostr events with proven NIP-77 integration; reinventing it is a year of work that we will not recoup.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3657:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3513:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:2872:    23	| Per-relay provenance (which relays delivered each event; first seen / last seen) | Out of scope; the crate models events, not their wire history | `subsystems.md` §7.1 "Duplicate id → merge relay provenance set"; ADR-0007 diagnostics; outbox routing scoring in M2+ |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3660:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3516:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9fead0e.md:3011:   162	On duplicate-id insert (§7.1 row 2), `insert()` does not touch the primary; it only updates the provenance sidecar (`last_seen_ms` bump on the matching `ProvenanceEntry`, or append). The "primary relay" — for outbox-routing scoring (M2) and ADR-0007 diagnostics — is deterministically the first relay observed (`sources[0]` after sort by `first_seen_ms`).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3680:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3566:docs/plan/principles.md:9:5. **The kernel never grows app nouns.** ADR-0009 doctrine D0 is enforced by review and by the [M11](m11-podcast.md) podcast-app proof.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3709:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3923:docs/design/framework-magic/intro.md:56:1. **It forces honesty.** If a chapter cannot fill the "App writes" field with `"nothing"` or a single safe call, the framework has leaked the operation to the app, and the doctrine D0 boundary is violated. The author of that chapter is required to file an ADR rather than ship the bullet as-is.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3743:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4745:docs/plan/subsystem-matrix.md:12:| **Non-Nostr data bridge** | [M0](m0-fixture.md) (substrate), [M10](m10-blossom.md) (long-running capabilities), [M11](m11-podcast.md) (podcast app proves it in production) | DomainModule trait + ADR-0007 bridge lanes; first proven by fixture-todo-core; production proof in podcast app. |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3744:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4782:docs/plan/principles.md:9:5. **The kernel never grows app nouns.** ADR-0009 doctrine D0 is enforced by review and by the [M11](m11-podcast.md) podcast-app proof.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3750:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4920:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:8:| 2026-05-18 01:30 | 0a | Advisor pass: broadcast safe-rebase-push protocol to all 6 running agents (avoid push race on master). T1 description updated to mandate worktree isolation + rebase-push protocol. Heartbeat cron rewritten (job 811003f1) with stronger triage rules (design→review→impl chain; M11 gated on M10.5 *empirical* pass, not just designed; debt triage uses both must-fix and ADR-defer lanes; DerivedData sprawl mitigation; orphan detection). Heartbeat runtime is session-only (durable flag ignored). 6 stale stashes from prior codex sessions dropped. |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3751:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4921:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:9:| 2026-05-18 01:55 | 1 | Wave 1 completed in ~30 min. Landed: T7 build-verifier f1e374b (all gates green, sim screenshot captured); T6 debt-auditor d660735 (0 TODO/FIXME, 0 unimplemented in FFI surface, 4 must-fix safety-comment items); T2 m2-designer 031fc07 (subscription compilation spec, 8 files); T3 m3-designer 9fead0e (LMDB schema spec, 6 files); T5 m11-designer 0dfb975 (M11 podcast design, 13 files); T4 m105-designer's ffi-hardening files (5) absorbed into orchestrator commit fcf8b43. Three codex post-merge reviews ran: review-1 on the wave-1 cumulative diff flagged 5 issues (debt-inventory exit-ready language, NIP-XX placeholder, ADR-deferral escape, file sizes); review-2 on fcf8b43 flagged 6 issues (D5 mismatch, pre-merge CI gate, freed-pointer UB, S10 conditional, file sizes again, symbol count); review-3 on 0dfb975 flagged 7 issues (Swift file sizes 898/642, MCP-in-CI, AudioPlayback policy, EmbeddingId cycle, rig.rs weakened, OPML pixel-parity, view count). All flagged issues are being addressed via dedicated fix-it tasks T8 (codex-fixer-1) and T11 (codex-fixer-2). T1 (m1-hardener) unblocked + dispatched. T9 (ffi-safety-commenter) dispatched for the 4 must-fix items. Post-merge codex review protocol established and entered memory. |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3752:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4922:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:10:| 2026-05-18 02:10 | 2 | Heartbeat #2. Landed: T9 ffi-safety-commenter 03d7a23 (SAFETY comments accurate, semantics unchanged, cargo+tests pass; codex flagged pre-existing clippy lints in actor.rs+reactivity-bench → T12); m1-hardener live.rs prototype stashed (was polluting main tree again). Two outstanding codex reviews caught up: codex on 031fc07 found 8 substantive M2-design issues (no-publish-to-indexer, D4 single-writer, D0 module ownership, plan-id churn, lane modeling) → T13; codex on 9fead0e found 10 substantive M3-design issues (atomicity ADR blocker, D2 not enforced through trait, GC pinned-overlay unbounded, kind:5 a-tag deletes missing, tombstone semantics, key encoding mismatches, scan_by_kind_dtag missing from trait) → T14. Worktree agent-a41054fd04781ce06 cleaned. T10 (plan.md split) dispatched. 4 agents in flight: m1-hardener, codex-fixer-1, codex-fixer-2, plan-splitter. |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3753:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4923:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:11:| 2026-05-18 02:25 | 3 | Heartbeat #3. Committed as 9eace68 (`plan/scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md`). Four parallel Opus explorers dispatched: T15 applesauce-explorer (`/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce`), T16 ndk-explorer (`/Users/pablofernandez/Work/NDK-nhlteu` + prior outbox-research/), T17 framework-magic-designer, T18 highlighter-explorer (`/Users/pablofernandez/Work/hl/app` + NIP-29). T8 codex-fixer-1 + T11 codex-fixer-2 both finished (commits fb139ab, f68a479, b4b6afa, 151c80c, d871767, 80217cc — five sequential codex-driven fix cycles). T8 worktree cleaned (acdef1454b384c4b2). DISK PRESSURE: master Volumes/Data hit 96%; purged 6 stale `Podcastr-*` DerivedData dirs from prior podcast-rmp sessions (recovered ~7GB); m1-hardener notified to use `-derivedDataPath` going forward. T12 (clippy clean) dispatched (clippy-cleaner). Holding T13/T14 (codex fixes for M2/M3 designs) until current wave settles to avoid rebase storm. 7 agents in flight: m1-hardener, plan-splitter, applesauce-explorer, ndk-explorer, framework-magic-designer, highlighter-explorer, clippy-cleaner. User asleep — autonomous mode armed (cron f7c9fecf). |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3754:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4924:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:12:| 2026-05-18 02:55 | 4 | Heartbeat #4 (autonomous). MAJOR: T17 framework-magic-designer landed edc17b0 (13-bullet contract + 14 named tests in framework_magic_contract.rs across 10 sub-files + index). T15 applesauce-explorer + T16 ndk-explorer both completed but couldn't write/commit themselves; orchestrator transcribed 13 files (5 applesauce + 8 ndk) and pushed as 8d633e8. KEY FINDING from NDK research that propagated into framework-magic contract + scope memo: "auto-update of open subs when follow list changes — no app code involved" is empirically FALSE in core NDK; Svelte gets it via runes, React requires explicit deps, raw core has NO follow-list watcher. NMP must build this as framework code (~200 LOC per platform) AND the framework-magic contract guarantees app dispatches zero code. T8 codex-fixer-1 also wrapped up its iterative cycle (fb139ab + 80217cc final). User landed mid-tick directive: maintain top-level README.md with TL;DR + decisions + architecture map (committed 810d0f8); heartbeat cron rewritten (cc1fba11) to fold README refresh into every tick. ~~4 lingering m11-design followup commits~~ (59f8d1b/8302cc1/fd8f173/2477372) from codex-fixer-2 cleaned by its own internal codex loop. T15/T16/T17 marked completed. Pending T13 (M2 codex fixes), T14 (M3 codex fixes) still held. Plan-splitter, m1-hardener, highlighter-explorer, codex-fixer-1, clippy-cleaner still in flight. |

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3778:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5066:docs/product-spec/api-surface.md:48:`AppConfig` is a `uniffi::Record` containing only platform-resolved primitives (paths, lists of relay URLs, feature-flag booleans). docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3785:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5104:docs/perf/codex-reviews/d660735.md:1025:d660735:docs/product-spec/cli-toolchain-phasing.md:170:- **AppState.** A `uniffi::Record` carrying everything the UI needs to render. Cloned across FFI.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3786:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5105:docs/perf/codex-reviews/d660735.md:1067:d660735:docs/product-spec/subsystems.md:306:`WalletState` is a `uniffi::Record` field of `AppState`. Wallet attachment is an action; payment is an action; receipt verification is automatic. No wallet UI hook mutates proof, token, nutzap, or transaction state directly; those records live in the actor-owned domain store and transition through the action ledger.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3900:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5352:docs/perf/codex-reviews/ab632c1.md:2496:- **Kind:3 auto-tracking** for the active account's follow list. When a kind:3 lands for the active account, the planner automatically refreshes the author-set of every subscription that depends on "the current user's follows". The app never re-issues the REQ — the framework recompiles transparently.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3946:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5407:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:193:README.md:21:- **Framework-magic contract** (kind:3 auto-tracking, `bunker://` URL onboarding, new-nsec creation, outbox-by-default-on-publish, etc.): designed ([docs/design/framework-magic.md](docs/design/framework-magic.md)) with 13 behaviors and 14 named tests in `crates/nmp-testing/tests/framework_magic_contract.rs`.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3955:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5416:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:202:README.md:38:- **Kind:3 auto-tracking is framework-magic** — when the active account's follow list changes, every open subscription that depends on "current user's follows" auto-recompiles on the wire. Apps dispatch zero code. The Applesauce/NDK research (`docs/research/`) confirmed core NDK does NOT provide this automatically — NMP must build it as framework code.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3965:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5426:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:212:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:7:| 2026-05-18 01:24 | 0 | Session start. Pulled to e9cbafa. Plan revised (55dd5f2) inserting M10.5 (FFI hardening) and concretizing M11 (`../podcast` rebuild). 15-min cron heartbeat armed. First wave of 6 background agents dispatched: build-verifier (T7), debt-auditor (T6), m2-designer (T2), m3-designer (T3), m105-designer (T4), m11-designer (T5). T1 blocked on T7. |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3966:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5427:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:216:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:12:| 2026-05-18 02:55 | 4 | Heartbeat #4 (autonomous). MAJOR: T17 framework-magic-designer landed edc17b0 (13-bullet contract + 14 named tests in framework_magic_contract.rs across 10 sub-files + index). T15 applesauce-explorer + T16 ndk-explorer both completed but couldn't write/commit themselves; orchestrator transcribed 13 files (5 applesauce + 8 ndk) and pushed as 8d633e8. KEY FINDING from NDK research that propagated into framework-magic contract + scope memo: "auto-update of open subs when follow list changes — no app code involved" is empirically FALSE in core NDK; Svelte gets it via runes, React requires explicit deps, raw core has NO follow-list watcher. NMP must build this as framework code (~200 LOC per platform) AND the framework-magic contract guarantees app dispatches zero code. T8 codex-fixer-1 also wrapped up its iterative cycle (fb139ab + 80217cc final). User landed mid-tick directive: maintain top-level README.md with TL;DR + decisions + architecture map (committed 810d0f8); heartbeat cron rewritten (cc1fba11) to fold README refresh into every tick. ~~4 lingering m11-design followup commits~~ (59f8d1b/8302cc1/fd8f173/2477372) from codex-fixer-2 cleaned by its own internal codex loop. T15/T16/T17 marked completed. Pending T13 (M2 codex fixes), T14 (M3 codex fixes) still held. Plan-splitter, m1-hardener, highlighter-explorer, codex-fixer-1, clippy-cleaner still in flight. |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3967:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5428:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:231:docs/perf/pending-user-decisions.md:13:**Decision (autonomous):** The canonical D0–D5 is what's in `docs/product-spec/overview-and-dx.md` §1.5 (kernel-boundary, best-effort, negentropy-first, outbox-automatic, single-writer-per-fact, snapshots-bounded). I corrected the README to match (commit pending in next push).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3968:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5429:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:237:docs/perf/pending-user-decisions.md:24:**Current default while you decide:** option (a). README + framework-magic docs will be aligned to product-spec D0–D5; supporting rules cited by their source (RMP-bible invariant N, ADR-NNNN, etc.).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:3993:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5454:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:3555:docs/design/framework-magic.md:73:The orchestrator's heartbeat triage rule includes a "framework-magic regression" gate: any milestone landing that *removes* a bullet without an ADR fails review.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4014:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5475:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:4546:    19	- **Kind:3 auto-tracking** for the active account's follow list. When a kind:3 lands for the active account, the planner automatically refreshes the author-set of every subscription that depends on "the current user's follows". The app never re-issues the REQ — the framework recompiles transparently.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4024:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5485:docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:1042:+Search confirmed: no code in `core/` watches `session.followSet`, watches any "follows changed" event, or mutates `subscription.filters.authors` on the fly. docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4189:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5650:docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:7234:docs/perf/pending-user-decisions.md:24:**Current default while you decide:** option (a). docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4197:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5658:docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:7242:README.md:33:  Three additional load-bearing rules are not D-numbered but are equally binding (RMP-bible invariants from [`docs/aim.md` §2](docs/aim.md)): **errors never cross FFI** (operational failures become `toast: Option<String>` state fields), **capabilities report; never decide policy** (native bridges execute and report; Rust decides retry/recovery), and the **reactivity contract** (composite reverse index · ≤60 Hz/view · working-set bounded; ADR-0001..0004).

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4268:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5729:docs/perf/codex-reviews/edc17b0.md:1216:docs/perf/orchestration-log.md:12:| 2026-05-18 02:55 | 4 | Heartbeat #4 (autonomous). MAJOR: T17 framework-magic-designer landed edc17b0 (13-bullet contract + 14 named tests in framework_magic_contract.rs across 10 sub-files + index). T15 applesauce-explorer + T16 ndk-explorer both completed but couldn't write/commit themselves; orchestrator transcribed 13 files (5 applesauce + 8 ndk) and pushed as 8d633e8. KEY FINDING from NDK research that propagated into framework-magic contract + scope memo: "auto-update of open subs when follow list changes — no app code involved" is empirically FALSE in core NDK; Svelte gets it via runes, React requires explicit deps, raw core has NO follow-list watcher. NMP must build this as framework code (~200 LOC per platform) AND the framework-magic contract guarantees app dispatches zero code. T8 codex-fixer-1 also wrapped up its iterative cycle (fb139ab + 80217cc final). User landed mid-tick directive: maintain top-level README.md with TL;DR + decisions + architecture map (committed 810d0f8); heartbeat cron rewritten (cc1fba11) to fold README refresh into every tick. ~~4 lingering m11-design followup commits~~ (59f8d1b/8302cc1/fd8f173/2477372) from codex-fixer-2 cleaned by its own internal codex loop. T15/T16/T17 marked completed. Pending T13 (M2 codex fixes), T14 (M3 codex fixes) still held. Plan-splitter, m1-hardener, highlighter-explorer, codex-fixer-1, clippy-cleaner still in flight. |

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4277:docs/perf/codex-reviews/810d0f8.md:5738:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9eace68.md:58:+- **Kind:3 auto-tracking** for the active account's follow list. When a kind:3 lands for the active account, the planner automatically refreshes the author-set of every subscription that depends on "the current user's follows". The app never re-issues the REQ — the framework recompiles transparently.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4653:   115	+| 4 | Recompilation triggers (kind:10002, view open/close, reconnect, account switch, manual, user-configured change) | recompilation.md |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4659:   121	+| 10 | Open questions for follow-up ADRs | this file (below) |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4663:   125	+These remain to be resolved by ADRs after design review, not in this design pass.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4665:   127	+1. **Plan-id stability under perturbation.** The intro/compiler picks "logical-interest set + author-mailbox snapshot ⇒ plan-id." That ties plan-id to mailbox membership, so a single new kind:10002 arrival reshuffles plan-ids for every interest including that author. An alternative scopes plan-id to the *logical-interest set only* and tracks per-relay assignment as a separate stable identifier. Pick one in an ADR; the test contracts in §9 assume the former.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4666:   128	+2. **Filter-merge lattice formal-isation.** §3 step 3 lists which filter fields are safely mergeable across logical interests (`authors`, `kinds`, `since`, `until`, `limit`, tag operators). It does not yet specify the merge algebra for the corner case where two interests differ only in `limit` but agree on authors and kinds. Worth an ADR-level note before the lattice is coded; `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.2 promises "a formal merge lattice for `limit`, `since`, `until`, multi-filter arrays, and tag operators."
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4667:   129	+3. **Per-author indexer-fallback ledger row?** Today the compiler treats indexer fallback as an inline relay assignment. If the kind:10002 fetch is its own durable action (M6 ledger), the fallback becomes a tracked action with retry/cancel semantics. Cleaner for diagnostics; heavier for M2. Defer.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4668:   130	+4. **Read-relay vs write-relay use for subscriptions on the same author.** NIP-65 defines write relays (outbox) for the author's own events and read relays (inbox) for events directed *at* the author. For a `Timeline { authors: [...] }` we want write relays. For a `Notifications { p: [author] }` we want inbox relays. The compiler distinguishes them by filter shape (`authors` vs `#p`), matching the `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.3 routing table. Document a corner case: kind-1 filtered by both `authors` and `#p` is rare but real (replies to the author from the author). Pick a precedence in an ADR; current bias is `authors` wins (write relays).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4669:   131	+5. **User-configured relay precedence vs NIP-65.** A user adds `wss://my-private.example` to local config. Does it *augment* (union) or *override* (replace) NIP-65 routing for the active account? `subsystems.md` §7.3 default-resolves by NIP-65; user-configured is "fallback" in the indexer sense. ADR needs to spell out the augment/override question for the active account specifically.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4671:   133	+7. **NSE crate compilation surface.** `nmp-nip17-nse` (M9) runs in iOS Notification Service Extension with bounded memory; it needs to compile a single-author single-relay plan without the full planner. Confirm in an ADR that the compiler exposes a `compile_one(spec, mailbox_cache_snapshot) -> Plan` pure function suitable for NSE use, and that the function does not require a live actor.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4755:   217	+| Neither populated | (handled by stage 3 as "use active-account read relays") | — |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4844:    82	| Neither populated | (handled by stage 3 as "use active-account read relays") | — |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4886:   124	a REQ; relay-count optimization is open question 8 (future ADR).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4900:   138	    pub canonical_filter_hash: String,         // for ADR-0007 WireSubscriptionStatus
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4954:   192	| `open_firehose_tag` (requests.rs:170-200) | Registers one `LogicalInterest { shape: { kinds: [1], tags: { #t: [tag] } }, scope: ActiveAccount, lifecycle: Tailing }`. Routes to active-account read relays per §3.1 table. |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4973:   211	- It does not move the action ledger into M2 — `SendNote` lands in M6.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4977:   215	- It does not move social bootstrap (follow-list timeline, account kind:0/3/10002 fetches)
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4993:     6	This section defines the **publish-side seam** the M2 milestone lands so the M6 write path has a ready surface. There is no publish code in the repo today (`crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/requests.rs` contains no `EVENT` outbound; the relay worker has no publish channel). M2 lands the trait and the override action; M6 writes the first concrete consumer (`SendNoteAction`).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:4995:     8	This is the framing the parent index calls out as "design seam now, first concrete consumer in M6." Without this seam, M6 risks reinventing outbox routing inline.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5098:   111	    pub active_account:   Option<AccountId>,
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5168:   181	  NoAuthorRelays }` in the action ledger, rendered as a toast per ADR-0007's `SideEffect` lane.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5215:     4	> Read first: ADR-0007 (`docs/decisions/0007-diagnostics-and-non-nostr-domain-data.md`) — every record here extends ADR-0007 types; it does not replace them.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5217:     6	The compiler's routing decisions are the most subtle correctness surface in the M2 milestone. They are also the easiest to silently get wrong (`docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §3, "automatic behaviour also needs strong tests"). Diagnostics make the four sources of relay knowledge legible — separately, never collapsed.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5253:    42	Each lane has one record type. All four are exposed to the platform via the existing ADR-0007 `ViewBatch` lane (low-cadence, coalesced to 1–4 Hz per ADR-0007 "How status crosses the bridge"). They feed into the diagnostics screen, not into normal product UI.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5317:   106	Emitted by `handle_event` (`crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/ingest.rs:134-164`) for every EVENT arrival. This is the highest-cardinality lane and the only one where coalescing matters at the ADR-0007 boundary: the platform diagnostic view consumes a summarised projection (`ProvenanceSummary` per author or per event), not the raw fact stream.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5346:   135	The compiler may *use* facts from multiple lanes to compute a routing decision; it may **not** present them as one. Concretely:
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5348:   137	- **Routing decision** (`AuthorRouting.source` from [compiler.md](compiler.md) §3.1): records *which lane* the relay set was derived from. Single-valued; one of `Nip65 | Hint | Indexer | UserConfigured`. The author may have facts in three lanes; the compiler picks one, says so, and the other lane records remain visible.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5360:   149	- `User-configured` is the **only** lane the compiler treats as authoritative-by-policy (the user said so). Open question 5 in the parent index resolves the augment-vs-override precedence between NIP-65 and user-configured for the active account.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5362:   151	The lesson the four-lane discipline preserves: routing is **derivable but contested** evidence. Collapsing the lanes loses information; preserving them lets the diagnostic answer "why did we route this REQ to that relay?" months after the decision.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5413:   202	3. `UserConfiguredRelayFact` records — increments/decrements `by_lane.user_configured` per (relay_url) membership. Records with `category == Indexer` contribute to `by_lane.indexer_fallback` (a sub-count of `user_configured`); the UI can show both the aggregate and the Indexer-specific sub-total.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5423:   212	Emission cadence follows ADR-0007's diagnostic-view rule: material-transition immediately, otherwise 1–4 Hz. The provenance counter ticks every second; the `by_lane` counts only emit on `CompiledPlan` recompiles or new mailbox arrivals.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5427:   216	Per `docs/aim.md` §4.4 ("the developer does not pick relays per operation; the framework does") and ADR-0007's domain-of-diagnostics separation, end-user product UIs do not show "relay X is serving 12 authors." That is operator/debug surface. Normal apps consume the `LogicalInterestStatus` summaries; `RelayCoveragePayload` is for the diagnostics screen (proof iOS app screenshot in `docs/perf/m2/outbox-routing.md` per [`docs/plan/m2-subscription-compilation.md`](../../plan/m2-subscription-compilation.md)).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5435:docs/design/subscription-compilation/diagnostics.md:137:- **Routing decision** (`AuthorRouting.source` from [compiler.md](compiler.md) §3.1): records *which lane* the relay set was derived from. Single-valued; one of `Nip65 | Hint | Indexer | UserConfigured`. The author may have facts in three lanes; the compiler picks one, says so, and the other lane records remain visible.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5437:docs/design/subscription-compilation/diagnostics.md:202:3. `UserConfiguredRelayFact` records — increments/decrements `by_lane.user_configured` per (relay_url) membership. Records with `category == Indexer` contribute to `by_lane.indexer_fallback` (a sub-count of `user_configured`); the UI can show both the aggregate and the Indexer-specific sub-total.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5440:docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md:97:Outbox routing implication: if `pubkey` was previously routed to the indexer fallback set (Stage 2 read-fallback), the compiler now reassigns to the author's declared write relays. The wire-emitter closes the indexer REQ for that author's slice and opens a new REQ on the declared relays. ADR-0007 diagnostics reflect the route source flipping from `UserConfigured::Indexer` to `Nip65`.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5469:     5	> **Companion docs:** [`docs/plan/m2-subscription-compilation.md`](../plan/m2-subscription-compilation.md); `docs/aim.md` §4.4–§4.5; `docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §7; ADR-0007 (diagnostic lanes); `docs/design/kernel-substrate.md` §3 (`ViewModule`) and §4 (`ActionModule`); `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.2–§7.3.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5489:    25	| 4 | Recompilation triggers (kind:10002, view open/close, reconnect, account switch, manual, user-configured change) | recompilation.md |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5495:    31	| 10 | Open questions for follow-up ADRs | this file (below) |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5499:    35	These remain to be resolved by ADRs after design review, not in this design pass.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5506:    42	2. **Filter-merge lattice formal-isation.** §3 step 3 lists which filter fields are safely mergeable across logical interests (`authors`, `kinds`, `since`, `until`, `limit`, tag operators). It does not yet specify the merge algebra for the corner case where two interests differ only in `limit` but agree on authors and kinds. Worth an ADR-level note before the lattice is coded; `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.2 promises "a formal merge lattice for `limit`, `since`, `until`, multi-filter arrays, and tag operators."
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5507:    43	3. **Per-author indexer-fallback ledger row?** Today the compiler treats indexer fallback as an inline relay assignment. If the kind:10002 fetch is its own durable action (M6 ledger), the fallback becomes a tracked action with retry/cancel semantics. Cleaner for diagnostics; heavier for M2. Defer.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5508:    44	4. **Read-relay vs write-relay use for subscriptions on the same author.** NIP-65 defines write relays (outbox) for the author's own events and read relays (inbox) for events directed *at* the author. For a `Timeline { authors: [...] }` we want write relays. For a `Notifications { p: [author] }` we want inbox relays. The compiler distinguishes them by filter shape (`authors` vs `#p`), matching the `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.3 routing table. Document a corner case: kind-1 filtered by both `authors` and `#p` is rare but real (replies to the author from the author). Pick a precedence in an ADR; current bias is `authors` wins (write relays).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5509:    45	5. **User-configured relay precedence vs NIP-65.** A user adds `wss://my-private.example` to local config. Does it *augment* (union) or *override* (replace) NIP-65 routing for the active account? `subsystems.md` §7.3 default-resolves by NIP-65; user-configured is "fallback" in the indexer sense. ADR needs to spell out the augment/override question for the active account specifically.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5511:    47	7. **NSE crate compilation surface.** `nmp-nip17-nse` (M9) runs in iOS Notification Service Extension with bounded memory; it needs to compile a single-author single-relay plan without the full planner. Confirm in an ADR that the compiler exposes a `compile_one(spec, mailbox_cache_snapshot) -> Plan` pure function suitable for NSE use, and that the function does not require a live actor.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5787:    40	`nmp-nip65` implements exactly one extension trait family: `ViewModule`. It does *not* implement `ActionModule` (kind:10002 publish is the user's own "update my relay list" action, deferred to M6's action ledger; in this milestone it has no write surface). It does *not* implement `DomainModule` (mailbox records live in the kernel-owned mailbox cache; they are queryable Nostr events, not durable app-defined records).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5965:   218	- **No publish action.** Updating a user's own kind:10002 is `nmp-nip01::UpdateRelayList` (M6); that action depends on `nmp-nip65::parse` to validate the local copy before publishing.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5966:   219	- **No outbox routing policy.** The decision "publish goes to author write relays + recipient inbox relays" is the publish planner ([outbox.md](outbox.md) §7), not this crate. This crate provides the lookups; the policy lives in `nmp-core::kernel::planner::publish`.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5968:   221	- **No FFI types.** `MailboxesPayload` is exposed at FFI via the per-app generated enum (per ADR-0010 codegen); the crate itself ships pure Rust.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6090:docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md:97:Outbox routing implication: if `pubkey` was previously routed to the indexer fallback set (Stage 2 read-fallback), the compiler now reassigns to the author's declared write relays. docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6091:docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md:105:Batching contract: the actor's planner inbox coalesces consecutive `ViewOpened` triggers within one actor tick into a single recompile pass. This is the existing reactivity batching (`docs/design/reactivity/scheduling-and-data-model.md`) extended to the planner; the M2 implementation respects the same `≤60Hz/view` budget from ADR-0002 by capping recompiles at one per tick regardless of trigger fan-in.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6100:docs/design/subscription-compilation/outbox.md:265:This is the SideEffect-lane payload per ADR-0007. The platform diagnostic UI renders an entry on every override; the count over a session is a soft metric (Cargo dependents using overrides routinely should re-examine their flow).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6101:docs/design/subscription-compilation/outbox.md:280:- **Concurrent publish coalescing** — if two actions publish the same event (a republish), the planner can dedupe to one wire EVENT per relay. Defer to M6 / M7 stress test.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6102:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:19:The summary diagnosis: **the planner is a string formatter, not a compiler.** Every REQ is a per-call-site decision; routing is one of two literals; recompilation is impossible because nothing is compiled. The diagnostics in `crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/mod.rs:117-154` already type `RelayStatus` / `WireSubscriptionStatus` / `LogicalInterestStatus` per ADR-0007 — but the planner currently emits at most one `LogicalInterestStatus` per view kind because there is no logical-interest object to scope it against.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6103:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:127:- A **wire REQ**. Wire REQs are produced by the compiler; they live in `WireSubscriptionStatus` per ADR-0007.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6104:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:129:- A **diagnostic record**. ADR-0007 lanes are facts derived from the planner's state, not inputs.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6105:docs/design/subscription-compilation.md:5:> **Companion docs:** [`docs/plan/m2-subscription-compilation.md`](../plan/m2-subscription-compilation.md); `docs/aim.md` §4.4–§4.5; `docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §7; ADR-0007 (diagnostic lanes); `docs/design/kernel-substrate.md` §3 (`ViewModule`) and §4 (`ActionModule`); `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.2–§7.3.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6108:docs/design/subscription-compilation.md:35:These remain to be resolved by ADRs after design review, not in this design pass.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6110:docs/design/subscription-compilation.md:42:2. **Filter-merge lattice formal-isation.** §3 step 3 lists which filter fields are safely mergeable across logical interests (`authors`, `kinds`, `since`, `until`, `limit`, tag operators). It does not yet specify the merge algebra for the corner case where two interests differ only in `limit` but agree on authors and kinds. Worth an ADR-level note before the lattice is coded; `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.2 promises "a formal merge lattice for `limit`, `since`, `until`, multi-filter arrays, and tag operators."
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6111:docs/design/subscription-compilation.md:43:3. **Per-author indexer-fallback ledger row?** Today the compiler treats indexer fallback as an inline relay assignment. If the kind:10002 fetch is its own durable action (M6 ledger), the fallback becomes a tracked action with retry/cancel semantics. Cleaner for diagnostics; heavier for M2. Defer.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6112:docs/design/subscription-compilation.md:44:4. **Read-relay vs write-relay use for subscriptions on the same author.** NIP-65 defines write relays (outbox) for the author's own events and read relays (inbox) for events directed *at* the author. For a `Timeline { authors: [...] }` we want write relays. For a `Notifications { p: [author] }` we want inbox relays. The compiler distinguishes them by filter shape (`authors` vs `#p`), matching the `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.3 routing table. Document a corner case: kind-1 filtered by both `authors` and `#p` is rare but real (replies to the author from the author). Pick a precedence in an ADR; current bias is `authors` wins (write relays).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6113:docs/design/subscription-compilation.md:45:5. **User-configured relay precedence vs NIP-65.** A user adds `wss://my-private.example` to local config. Does it *augment* (union) or *override* (replace) NIP-65 routing for the active account? `subsystems.md` §7.3 default-resolves by NIP-65; user-configured is "fallback" in the indexer sense. ADR needs to spell out the augment/override question for the active account specifically.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6114:docs/design/subscription-compilation.md:47:7. **NSE crate compilation surface.** `nmp-nip17-nse` (M9) runs in iOS Notification Service Extension with bounded memory; it needs to compile a single-author single-relay plan without the full planner. Confirm in an ADR that the compiler exposes a `compile_one(spec, mailbox_cache_snapshot) -> Plan` pure function suitable for NSE use, and that the function does not require a live actor.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6511:    99	    /// recompilation triggers that the view module opts into (account-switch,
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6518:   106	`InterestContext` exposes read access to the mailbox cache and to the active account but **not** to the relay set; the view module never names relays. That keeps `nmp-aim.md` doctrine 5 ("outbox routing automatic; manual relay selection is the opt-out, not the default") structurally enforced — there is no place for a view module to write a URL.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6531:   119	The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in [`docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`](../../plan/m8-multi-account.md) — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6533:   121	Account-scoped interests with empty `authors` and empty `#p` (e.g. a free-form hashtag firehose) resolve against the active account's *read relays* (NIP-65 read side) — the user's own subscription preferences, not a globally hardcoded relay. Today's `firehose_requests()` at `crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/requests.rs:357-372` hardcodes `RelayRole::Content`; under the compiler this becomes "active-account read relays, falling back to indexer set if the active account has no kind:10002."
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6539:   127	- A **wire REQ**. Wire REQs are produced by the compiler; they live in `WireSubscriptionStatus` per ADR-0007.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6541:   129	- A **diagnostic record**. ADR-0007 lanes are facts derived from the planner's state, not inputs.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6549:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:12:- **The seam that emits REQs is parameterized by `RelayRole`.** `crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/requests.rs:530-556` (`req()`) inserts a `WireSub { role, .. }` keyed by a string sub-id and emits `OutboundMessage { role, text }`. The role *is* the routing decision; there is no relay-URL field on `WireSub` or `OutboundMessage`. Any compiler that fans an interest out across N URLs has to replace this helper.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6554:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:17:- **No publish path exists yet.** `crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/requests.rs:30` (no occurrences of `EVENT` outbound) and `crates/nmp-core/src/relay.rs:42-45` (`OutboundMessage` carries only role + text). The first publish action (M6 `SendNote`) will hit this same `req()`-style seam. M2 must establish the planner shape before M6 builds the first user of it; the doctrine "no developer-supplied relays for a publish" (`docs/aim.md` §6 doctrine 5; `docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.3 row "Publish leaked to wrong relays") needs a structural enforcement point.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6555:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:19:The summary diagnosis: **the planner is a string formatter, not a compiler.** Every REQ is a per-call-site decision; routing is one of two literals; recompilation is impossible because nothing is compiled. The diagnostics in `crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/mod.rs:117-154` already type `RelayStatus` / `WireSubscriptionStatus` / `LogicalInterestStatus` per ADR-0007 — but the planner currently emits at most one `LogicalInterestStatus` per view kind because there is no logical-interest object to scope it against.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6561:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:121:Account-scoped interests with empty `authors` and empty `#p` (e.g. a free-form hashtag firehose) resolve against the active account's *read relays* (NIP-65 read side) — the user's own subscription preferences, not a globally hardcoded relay. Today's `firehose_requests()` at `crates/nmp-core/src/kernel/requests.rs:357-372` hardcodes `RelayRole::Content`; under the compiler this becomes "active-account read relays, falling back to indexer set if the active account has no kind:10002."
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6576:docs/design/kernel-substrate.md:13:`nmp-core` defines five trait families. Each extension crate implements one or more of them. The kernel runtime knows nothing about a module's specific types — only that the module conforms to these traits and contributes variants to the generated per-app enums (per ADR-0010).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6592:docs/design/app-extension-kernel.md:213:This supports app-local agents, feedback identities, human accounts, external signers, and provider credential owners without adding "agent" or "feedback" semantics to `nmp-core`.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6611:- [compiler.md](/Users/pablofernandez/Work/nostr-multi-platform/docs/design/subscription-compilation/compiler.md:124): references “open question 8,” but the parent list has only 1–7. Either add the ADR item deliberately or remove the dangling new open question.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6628:- [compiler.md](/Users/pablofernandez/Work/nostr-multi-platform/docs/design/subscription-compilation/compiler.md:124): references “open question 8,” but the parent list has only 1–7. Either add the ADR item deliberately or remove the dangling new open question.

 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# ADR 0009: App extension kernel boundary
     2	
     3	**Date:** 2026-05-17
     4	**Status:** accepted
     5	**Adopts:** `docs/design/app-extension-kernel.md` (the design proposal)
     6	**Modifies:** ADR-0006 (slice positioning), ADR-0008 (Twitter clone repositioned as first extension module)
     7	**Companion ADR:** 0010 (resolves open question 1: generated app enum vs type-erased registry)
     8	
     9	## Context
    10	
    11	The product spec as originally written placed 15 social-client-shaped view kinds (Profile, Timeline, Thread, Reactions, Conversation, ...) directly in `nmp-core`, alongside a closed `AppAction` enum and a closed `AppUpdate` enum. The first developer to build something that isn't a social client — Highlighter, TENEX, Win the Day, Cut Tracker, podcast apps — has two bad options:
    12	
    13	- Add their app nouns (`Highlight`, `Project`, `Episode`, `WeightLog`, `DailyPlan`) to `nmp-core`. The framework becomes a junk drawer of every consumer's domain concepts. Untenable.
    14	- Build them in Swift/Kotlin. Violates the doctrine ("no business logic in native code") the framework exists to enforce.
    15	
    16	The proposal in `docs/design/app-extension-kernel.md` identifies this as a fundamental abstraction error. It argues for reframing NMP as a **Nostr-native app kernel with first-class extension modules** rather than a framework with closed built-ins.
    17	
    18	This ADR formally accepts that reframing.
    19	
    20	## Decision
    21	
    22	`nmp-core` provides **generic infrastructure only**:
    23	
    24	- Actor runtime and unidirectional state flow.
    25	- Verified Nostr event store with replaceable/delete/expiration semantics.
    26	- Subscription planner with composite-keyed reverse index.
    27	- Relay routing and publish pipeline.
    28	- Signer/session plumbing (identity-scope agnostic).
    29	- Domain-store substrate for non-Nostr records.
    30	- Typed view registry (driven by `ViewModule` trait).
    31	- Durable action ledger (driven by `ActionModule` trait).
    32	- Capability bridge (driven by `CapabilityModule` trait).
    33	- Platform-shadow + codegen machinery.
    34	- Diagnostics and test harnesses.
    35	
    36	`nmp-core` does **not** contain:
    37	
    38	- Profile, Timeline, Thread, Reactions, Conversation, or any other view-kind business logic.
    39	- Wallet, messaging, blossom, or any other domain feature.
    40	- A closed `AppAction` enum or `AppUpdate` enum.
    41	- A closed `ViewSpec` enum.
    42	- App-specific identity concepts (agent, feedback identity, coach, etc.).
    43	
    44	**Four layers, clear ownership.**
    45	
    46	| Layer | Owns | May contain app nouns? |
    47	|---|---|---|
    48	| `nmp-core` kernel | actor, store substrate, planner, ledger, registries, codegen, diagnostics | No |
    49	| NMP protocol modules (`nmp-nip01`, `nmp-nip17`, `nmp-nip29`, `nmp-nip65`, `nmp-blossom`, `nmp-nwc`, …) | reusable Nostr protocol concepts: Event, Filter, Keys, gift-wrap, groups, mailboxes, blossom, NWC | Only protocol nouns |
    50	| App core crate (`twitter-core`, `highlighter-core`, `tenex-core`, …) | app domain records, view modules, action modules, app-specific capability types, policies | Yes |
    51	| Platform shell | rendering, OS handle execution, generated wrappers | No policy nouns beyond UI labels |
    52	
    53	**Five extension trait families** (concrete signatures in `docs/design/kernel-substrate.md`):
    54	
    55	- `DomainModule` — durable non-Nostr records with migrations and indexes.
    56	- `ViewModule` — typed reactive projections with payloads and deltas.
    57	- `ActionModule` — durable workflows on the action ledger.
    58	- `CapabilityModule` — typed native fact reports.
    59	- `IdentityModule` — signer scopes beyond "active Nostr account."
    60	
    61	**The rule.** If implementing Highlighter, TENEX, Win the Day, Cut Tracker, or a podcast app requires adding domain nouns to `nmp-core`, the extension boundary is wrong and the kernel must change, not the app.
    62	
    63	## What changes from prior ADRs
    64	
    65	- **ADR-0006 (vertical-slice-first):** the slice's discipline survives — running code at every checkpoint, one architectural ingredient per sub-phase. The slice's *target* changes: the kind:0 Profile path is now built as the canonical `ViewModule` in a Nostr-protocol module, not as a built-in feature of `nmp-core`. The slice now proves the extension boundary first, then the protocol module on top.
    66	- **ADR-0008 (Twitter clone):** the Twitter clone is repositioned as **the first canonical extension module** demonstrating the kernel boundary at scale, not as the framework's set of built-in features. The sub-phase plan grows by one or two phases (extension-boundary prototype with a tiny non-Nostr fixture module lands before the Twitter slice begins).
    67	- **`product-spec.md` §4 (crate roster), §6.2–§6.6 (state/action/update/capabilities/views), §7 (most subsystem specs), §12 (phasing):** rewritten to reflect kernel + protocol-module + app-module layering. Built-in view kinds become "reference protocol modules with their own view modules." Built-in wallet / messages / blossom become protocol modules (`nmp-nwc`, `nmp-nip17`, `nmp-blossom`).
    68	- **`view-catalog.md`:** reframed as the catalog of *reference Nostr extension modules* shipped with the framework (Profile, Contacts, Timeline, Thread, Reactions, Conversation, ...). Apps can use them, ignore them, or replace them. They are not in `nmp-core`.
    69	
    70	## What survives intact
    71	
    72	The reactive machinery and platform shadow doctrine are independent of who owns the noun:
    73	
    74	- ADR-0001 (composite dependency keys): applies to any view module's `Dependencies` declaration.
    75	- ADR-0002 (per-view delta budget): applies to any view module's emitted deltas.
    76	- ADR-0003 (working-set memory): applies to the kernel's hot/cold split regardless of module count.
    77	- ADR-0004 (allocation measurement): applies to the kernel.
    78	- ADR-0005 (domain-keyed platform shadow): applies to any view module; per-module wrapper API is generated, not hand-written.
    79	- ADR-0007 (diagnostics bridge): applies to any extension's relay status, action ledger entries, domain records, and capability reports.
    80	
    81	The doctrines from `product-spec.md` §1.5 (D1 best-effort rendering, D2 negentropy first, D3 outbox automatic, D4 single writer per fact, D5 snapshots bounded by what's open) survive intact and apply across all modules.
    82	
    83	## Consequences
    84	
    85	- **Smaller kernel, larger ecosystem surface.** `nmp-core` shrinks substantially. The ecosystem grows: `nmp-nip01`, `nmp-nip02`, `nmp-nip17`, `nmp-nip25`, `nmp-nip29`, `nmp-nip65`, `nmp-nip77`, `nmp-blossom`, `nmp-nwc`, `nmp-cashu` become first-class protocol modules.
    86	- **Codegen is critical-path infrastructure.** Without `nmp gen modules` producing per-app concrete enums and platform wrappers, every app reinvents the EventBridge pattern. The codegen tool ships in v1.
    87	- **Phase 1a takes longer.** ADR-0008's 8-week estimate grows to roughly 12–15 weeks. The kernel substrate (1a.1) and tiny fixture module land before the first Nostr-shaped extension module. Twitter clone follows on top.
    88	- **Two fixture apps prove the boundary in v1.** A tiny non-Nostr-shaped fixture (e.g., a TODO/notes module with an app-local identity) lands in 1a.1; the Twitter clone is the first Nostr-shaped extension module. Together they prove the kernel works in both directions.
    89	- **The proof slices from the proposal (Highlighter-lite, Personal-coach-lite, TENEX-lite, Podcast-lite) become post-v1 demonstrations.** They are not v1 deliverables. They are evidence the boundary is right.
    90	- **Future protocol-spec evolution is cleaner.** A new NIP (say NIP-100) becomes a new crate, not a `nmp-core` patch.
    91	
    92	## Acceptance criteria for the boundary
    93	
    94	Verified by:
    95	
    96	1. The kernel-substrate prototype (Phase 1a.1) ships with a tiny non-Nostr fixture module demonstrating all five trait families. It compiles, runs in a reference shell, and contains no Nostr concepts.
    97	2. The Twitter clone (Phase 1a.2 onward) is implemented entirely as extension modules over `nmp-core` + protocol modules. `nmp-core` does not gain any of: `Profile`, `Timeline`, `Thread`, `Reactions`, `Conversation`, `Tweet`, `Compose` types or actions.
    98	3. A future hypothetical Highlighter-lite module can be added without changes to `nmp-core`. (Not built in v1; the design must support it.)
    99	
   100	## Alternatives considered
   101	
   102	- **Keep current spec; punt extension boundary to v2.** Rejected — shipping v1 with the wrong abstraction creates a major-version migration within a year. Worse than ~4 extra weeks of design now.
   103	- **Make `AppAction` / `ViewSpec` extensible via downcast / `Box<dyn Any>`.** Rejected — kills type safety at the FFI boundary; degrades into stringly-typed dispatch.
   104	- **Vendor in app-specific modules as cargo features on a monolithic `nmp` crate.** Rejected — explosive feature matrix; entangles compilation; doesn't solve UniFFI typing.
   105	- **String-keyed registry at runtime (open question 1's "type-erased" option).** Decided against in ADR-0010 in favor of generated app enum for compile-time safety. See that ADR for full reasoning.
   106	
   107	## Open questions resolved here vs deferred
   108	
   109	| Proposal open question | Resolution |
   110	|---|---|
   111	| 1. Generated app enum vs type-erased registry | Generated app enum — see ADR-0010 |
   112	| 2. Declarative vs Rust migrations for domain-module schemas | Resolved in `kernel-substrate.md` §3 — Rust migrations with a small declarative index API |
   113	| 3. UniFFI for app-level enums across crates | Resolved in `kernel-substrate.md` §5 — per-app `nmp-app-<name>` codegen crate owns the FFI exposure |
   114	| 4. Protocol modules as crates or features | Separate crates (`nmp-nip01`, `nmp-nip17`, ...). Sharper boundaries; explicit dep graph; testable in isolation |
   115	| 5. Minimum v1 extension API before social-client proof app | The 1a.1 substrate (5 trait families + codegen for one fixture) is the minimum. Twitter clone consumes it from 1a.2 onward |

 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# ADR 0007: Diagnostics and non-Nostr domain data over the app bridge
     2	
     3	**Date:** 2026-05-17
     4	**Status:** accepted
     5	
     6	## Context
     7	
     8	ADR-0005 defines the platform shadow: components read from typed, domain-keyed platform caches while Rust owns source-of-truth state and emits bounded deltas. ADR-0006 narrows the first runtime proof to one profile metadata vertical slice.
     9	
    10	Two adjacent questions must be settled before that slice grows:
    11	
    12	1. How can an app show what is happening at the networking level: relay connection state, wire subscriptions, logical view interests, cache coverage, reconnects, EOSE/CLOSED, and relay capabilities?
    13	2. How does non-Nostr data cross the same bridge: network monitor facts, wallet state, Blossom upload progress, sync jobs, push/decrypt status, local media imports, diagnostics, and capability results?
    14	
    15	Both surfaces are easy to get wrong. If relay status becomes raw socket callbacks, platform code starts owning protocol policy. If every diagnostic transition crosses FFI immediately, the status UI becomes its own firehose. If non-Nostr data is forced into fake Nostr events, the event store becomes polluted with records that do not obey Nostr semantics.
    16	
    17	## Decision
    18	
    19	Use the same actor-owned state model and `AppUpdate` bridge for diagnostics and non-Nostr data, but keep their domain records distinct from Nostr events.
    20	
    21	The platform receives:
    22	
    23	- Ordinary app state through `FullState`.
    24	- Bounded view/domain deltas through `ViewBatch`.
    25	- Ephemeral one-shot notifications through `SideEffect`.
    26	
    27	The platform never receives raw relay socket callbacks, raw planner callbacks, or raw capability callbacks as a parallel reactive channel. Native code may display status, but Rust owns status derivation, retry policy, subscription policy, and cache coverage policy.
    28	
    29	## Network observability model
    30	
    31	Networking is represented at three levels. Each level has stable identities, low-cardinality summaries, and optional debug detail.
    32	
    33	### 1. Relay status
    34	
    35	One record per relay URL known to the actor.
    36	
    37	```rust
    38	pub struct RelayStatus {
    39	    pub relay_url: String,
    40	    pub connection: RelayConnectionState,
    41	    pub auth: RelayAuthState,
    42	    pub capabilities: RelayCapabilities,
    43	    pub active_wire_subscriptions: u32,
    44	    pub reconnect_count: u32,
    45	    pub last_connected_at_ms: Option<u64>,
    46	    pub last_event_at_ms: Option<u64>,
    47	    pub last_notice: Option<String>,
    48	    pub last_error: Option<String>,
    49	    pub rtt_ms: Option<u32>,
    50	    pub bytes_rx: u64,
    51	    pub bytes_tx: u64,
    52	}
    53	
    54	pub enum RelayConnectionState {
    55	    Offline,
    56	    Connecting,
    57	    Connected,
    58	    BackingOff,
    59	    Closed,
    60	}
    61	
    62	pub enum RelayAuthState {
    63	    NotRequired,
    64	    ChallengeReceived,
    65	    Authenticating,
    66	    Authenticated,
    67	    Failed,
    68	}
    69	
    70	pub struct RelayCapabilities {
    71	    pub nip42_auth: CapabilityState,
    72	    pub nip77_negentropy: CapabilityState,
    73	}
    74	
    75	pub enum CapabilityState {
    76	    Unknown,
    77	    Supported,
    78	    Unsupported,
    79	}
    80	```
    81	
    82	Relay status answers: "Is this relay reachable, what can it do, and is it healthy?"
    83	
    84	### 2. Wire subscription status
    85	
    86	One record per actual REQ on an actual relay. This is the planner's network-level output after logical interests have been merged.
    87	
    88	```rust
    89	pub struct WireSubscriptionStatus {
    90	    pub wire_id: String,
    91	    pub relay_url: String,
    92	    pub canonical_filter_hash: String,
    93	    pub state: WireSubscriptionState,
    94	    pub logical_consumer_count: u32,
    95	    pub opened_at_ms: u64,
    96	    pub last_event_at_ms: Option<u64>,
    97	    pub eose_at_ms: Option<u64>,
    98	    pub close_reason: Option<CloseReason>,
    99	}
   100	
   101	pub enum WireSubscriptionState {
   102	    Opening,
   103	    Live,
   104	    Eose,
   105	    Closing,
   106	    Closed,
   107	    ClosedByRelay,
   108	    Retrying,
   109	}
   110	```
   111	
   112	Wire subscription status answers: "What did we actually ask the relay for?"
   113	
   114	### 3. Logical interest status
   115	
   116	One record per app-kernel interest: view, domain wrapper, monitor, pointer loader, sync job, or action dependency. This is the level app developers usually care about.
   117	
   118	```rust
   119	pub struct LogicalInterestStatus {
   120	    pub key: InterestKey,
   121	    pub state: LogicalInterestState,
   122	    pub refcount: u32,
   123	    pub view_ids: Vec<ViewId>,
   124	    pub relay_urls: Vec<String>,
   125	    pub cache_coverage: CacheCoverage,
   126	    pub backfill: Option<BackfillStatus>,
   127	    pub warming_until_ms: Option<u64>,
   128	}
   129	
   130	pub enum InterestKey {
   131	    Profile { pubkey: String },
   132	    Timeline { spec_hash: String },
   133	    Thread { root_event_id: String },
   134	    Action { action_id: String },
   135	    Monitor { monitor_id: String },
   136	    Sync { sync_id: String },
   137	}
   138	
   139	pub enum LogicalInterestState {
   140	    ServingCache,
   141	    Opening,
   142	    Tailing,
   143	    Backfilling,
   144	    Complete,
   145	    Partial,
   146	    Degraded,
   147	    BlockedNoRelays,
   148	    WarmClosing,
   149	    Closed,
   150	}
   151	```
   152	
   153	Logical interest status answers: "What is this screen/component/action waiting on, and is the answer complete, partial, degraded, or local-only?"
   154	
   155	## How status crosses the bridge
   156	
   157	Network diagnostics are actor state. They can appear in two forms:
   158	
   159	1. **Summary fields** in `AppState`, suitable for normal product UI:
   160	   - online/offline/degraded.
   161	   - number of connected relays.
   162	   - number of active subscriptions.
   163	   - number of running sync jobs.
   164	   - last user-visible network error.
   165	
   166	2. **Diagnostics views** opened explicitly by proof apps, debug UIs, and tests:
   167	   - `ViewSpec::NetworkDiagnostics`.
   168	   - `ViewSpec::RelayDiagnostics { relay_url }`.
   169	   - `ViewSpec::SubscriptionDiagnostics { interest_key }`.
   170	
   171	Diagnostics updates are coalesced separately from content views. They emit on material state transitions immediately, and otherwise at a low fixed cadence, initially 1 to 4 Hz. They do not emit once per event, once per byte counter change, or once per socket frame.
   172	
   173	`EmitDiagnosticSnapshot` remains the release-build escape hatch for high-detail state. It writes a JSON file and returns `Effect::DiagnosticReady { path }`, rather than streaming a huge diagnostic payload through normal UI state.
   174	
   175	## Vertical-slice implications
   176	
   177	The ADR-0006 profile slice implements the smallest useful subset:
   178	
   179	- One `RelayStatus` for the hardcoded relay.
   180	- One `LogicalInterestStatus::Profile { pubkey }`.

 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# ADR 0010: Generated per-app concrete enums at the FFI boundary
     2	
     3	**Date:** 2026-05-17
     4	**Status:** accepted
     5	**Resolves:** `docs/design/app-extension-kernel.md` open question 1
     6	**Depends on:** ADR-0009 (kernel boundary)
     7	
     8	## Context
     9	
    10	ADR-0009 establishes that NMP is a kernel with extension modules. Apps assemble themselves from a kernel (`nmp-core`), a set of protocol modules (`nmp-nip01`, `nmp-nip17`, ...), and an app-specific core crate (`twitter-core`, `highlighter-core`, ...). Each layer can define typed `AppAction` variants, `AppUpdate` variants, `ViewSpec` variants, and so on.
    11	
    12	The framework's existing FFI surface (UniFFI on iOS/Android, wasm-bindgen on web) expects **concrete, closed types**. Open enums don't exist in UniFFI. Two paths are possible:
    13	
    14	1. **Generated app enum.** Each app's build runs a codegen step that produces concrete per-app `AppAction = KernelAction | Nip01Action | Nip17Action | TwitterAction | ...` enums by composing variants from the kernel, the chosen protocol modules, and the app's own core crate. The FFI exposes the per-app concrete types. Compile-time type safety end-to-end.
    15	2. **Type-erased registry.** Modules register handlers keyed by string namespace; payloads cross FFI as `Vec<u8>` (or `serde_json::Value`). The kernel doesn't know module types statically; it dispatches via the registry. Plug-and-play module loading; loses type safety at the FFI boundary.
    16	
    17	This decision affects every layer of the framework.
    18	
    19	## Decision
    20	
    21	**Generated app enum.** Each app's build produces concrete per-app `AppAction`, `AppUpdate`, `ViewSpec`, `Capability*` enums via the `nmp gen modules` codegen step. UniFFI exposes the per-app concrete types via a thin per-app FFI crate (`nmp-app-<name>`).
    22	
    23	## How it works
    24	
    25	### App declaration
    26	
    27	An app declares its modules in `nmp.toml`:
    28	
    29	```toml
    30	[app]
    31	name = "twitter"
    32	display_name = "Twitter (nmp demo)"
    33	bundle_id = "com.example.nmp.twitter"
    34	
    35	[modules]
    36	kernel = "nmp-core"
    37	protocol = ["nmp-nip01", "nmp-nip02", "nmp-nip25", "nmp-nip10"]
    38	app = ["twitter-core"]
    39	
    40	[platforms]
    41	ios = true
    42	desktop = true
    43	```
    44	
    45	### Codegen output
    46	
    47	`nmp gen modules` produces a per-app crate `nmp-app-twitter/`:
    48	
    49	```
    50	nmp-app-twitter/
    51	├── Cargo.toml             # depends on nmp-core, the chosen protocol modules, twitter-core
    52	├── src/
    53	│   ├── lib.rs             # uniffi::setup_scaffolding!()
    54	│   ├── action.rs          # generated AppAction enum
    55	│   ├── update.rs          # generated AppUpdate enum
    56	│   ├── view_spec.rs       # generated ViewSpec enum
    57	│   ├── capability.rs      # generated capability traits
    58	│   ├── domain.rs          # generated domain registrations
    59	│   └── ffi.rs             # FfiApp wrapper exposing the concrete types
    60	├── uniffi.toml
    61	└── bindings/
    62	    ├── swift/             # generated Swift bindings checked in
    63	    ├── kotlin/            # generated Kotlin bindings checked in
    64	    └── typescript/        # generated TS bindings checked in
    65	```
    66	
    67	The generated `action.rs` looks like:
    68	
    69	```rust
    70	// AUTO-GENERATED — do not edit by hand. Regenerate with `nmp gen modules`.
    71	
    72	#[derive(Clone, uniffi::Enum)]
    73	pub enum AppAction {
    74	    // Kernel variants (from nmp-core)
    75	    Kernel(nmp_core::KernelAction),
    76	
    77	    // Protocol module variants
    78	    Nip01(nmp_nip01::Action),
    79	    Nip02(nmp_nip02::Action),
    80	    Nip25(nmp_nip25::Action),
    81	    Nip10(nmp_nip10::Action),
    82	
    83	    // App module variants
    84	    Twitter(twitter_core::Action),
    85	}
    86	
    87	impl AppAction {
    88	    pub fn dispatch(self, app: &Arc<FfiApp>) {
    89	        match self {
    90	            AppAction::Kernel(a) => app.core_tx.send(CoreMsg::Kernel(a)).ok(),
    91	            AppAction::Nip01(a) => app.core_tx.send(CoreMsg::Module("nip01", a.into_bytes())).ok(),
    92	            AppAction::Nip02(a) => app.core_tx.send(CoreMsg::Module("nip02", a.into_bytes())).ok(),
    93	            // ...
    94	        };
    95	    }
    96	}
    97	```
    98	
    99	`AppUpdate`, `ViewSpec`, and the capability traits are generated analogously.
   100	
   101	### How the actor dispatches
   102	
   103	Inside `nmp-core`'s actor, message handling is generic over module identifier:
   104	
   105	```rust
   106	enum CoreMsg {
   107	    Kernel(KernelAction),
   108	    Module(&'static str, Vec<u8>),    // module namespace + serialized action
   109	    Internal(InternalEvent),
   110	}
   111	
   112	fn handle_message(&mut self, msg: CoreMsg) {
   113	    match msg {
   114	        CoreMsg::Kernel(a) => self.handle_kernel_action(a),
   115	        CoreMsg::Module(ns, bytes) => {
   116	            let handler = self.module_registry.get(ns).expect("module not registered");
   117	            handler.dispatch(self, &bytes);
   118	        }
   119	        CoreMsg::Internal(e) => self.handle_internal(e),
   120	    }
   121	}
   122	```
   123	
   124	Each module registers itself with the kernel at startup, providing the `dispatch` closure that knows how to deserialize its own action type. The action bytes are produced by the generated FFI layer (which has typed access) and consumed by the module (which has typed access). The kernel in the middle is module-agnostic.
   125	
   126	### What UniFFI sees
   127	
   128	UniFFI sees only the generated per-app `nmp-app-twitter` crate. From its perspective, `AppAction` is a normal closed enum. Bindings regenerate deterministically.
   129	
   130	### Per-platform consumers
   131	
   132	A Swift app links `NmpAppTwitter.xcframework`. It sees Swift types `AppAction.kernel(...)`, `AppAction.nip01(...)`, `AppAction.twitter(...)`. The generated wrappers (`useTimeline`, `useProfile`, `@Twitter` property wrapper, etc.) come from the same codegen step.
   133	
   134	A Kotlin app links the `.so` and the generated Kotlin bindings. Same shape.
   135	
   136	A web app imports the generated wasm package. Same.
   137	
   138	## Trade-offs
   139	
   140	### What we get
   141	
   142	- **Compile-time type safety end-to-end.** Swift code can't construct an `AppAction.nip17(...)` if the app doesn't include `nmp-nip17`. The action variants for unused modules don't exist in that app's binary.
   143	- **Idiomatic enums per platform.** Swift `enum AppAction { case ... }`, Kotlin `sealed class AppAction`. Pattern-matchable.
   144	- **Bindings drift catchable.** CI regenerates bindings and diffs. A module change that produces incompatible bindings fails the build.
   145	- **Tree shaking.** Unused modules don't compile in; unused bindings don't ship.
   146	- **Cleaner FFI surface.** No `Vec<u8>` blobs across FFI. No JSON parsing on the platform side. No string-keyed switches.
   147	
   148	### What we lose / what costs
   149	
   150	- **Codegen is critical-path.** The `nmp gen modules` step must run before each build. CI must regenerate. App developers need to re-run after adding/removing modules. Mitigation: cargo `build.rs` can run it; precommit hooks; CI gate.
   151	- **Plug-and-play module loading at runtime is impossible.** Adding a module requires a rebuild. (This is fine — apps don't dynamically load modules at runtime.)
   152	- **Per-app FFI crate adds a build step.** Each app has its own `nmp-app-<name>` crate. Slight build-graph complexity. Mitigation: scaffolded by `nmp init` and `nmp add module`; developers rarely touch it.
   153	- **Binding regeneration churn.** Every module addition/removal regenerates bindings. Mitigation: CI handles it; checked-in bindings make diffs reviewable.
   154	
   155	## Why not type-erased
   156	
   157	The type-erased path (`Vec<u8>` actions, string-keyed registry, runtime dispatch) was seriously considered and rejected for v1:
   158	
   159	- **Loses safety where it matters most.** The FFI boundary is exactly where strong typing has the highest payoff. Swift / Kotlin / TypeScript consumers benefit from idiomatic typed enums. Vec<u8> + opaque dispatch reproduces JavaScript-shaped fragility in three statically-typed languages.
   160	- **Runtime errors replace compile-time errors.** Send the wrong-shape JSON action for a module → runtime failure. With generated enums → won't compile.
   161	- **Doesn't actually buy plug-and-play.** Apps don't load modules dynamically. They build with a chosen module set. The "plug-and-play" benefit is theoretical.
   162	- **Harder to debug.** Stringly-typed dispatch hides the actual shape of valid actions. Generated enums document themselves.
   163	
   164	If a future need for runtime module loading emerges (e.g., shipping a Highlighter "extension" that adds functionality to an existing app), it can be added as a layer on top of the generated enum (e.g., a `KernelAction::Plugin { namespace, bytes }` variant that intentionally falls back to type-erased dispatch for plugin code). The default path stays typed.
   165	
   166	## Consequences
   167	
   168	- The `nmp` CLI ships in v1 with `nmp gen modules` as the central command.
   169	- Every example app, the starter app, and the Twitter proof all run through `nmp gen modules` in their build pipeline.
   170	- A per-app FFI crate becomes the canonical FFI surface; raw `nmp-ffi` is for the kernel only.
   171	- Adding a new protocol module (`nmp-nip-XX`) becomes a standard pattern: create the crate, define the `Action`/`Update`/`ViewSpec`/`Capability` types, add to `nmp.toml`, run codegen.
   172	
   173	## Alternatives considered
   174	
   175	| Alternative | Why not |
   176	|---|---|
   177	| Type-erased registry (`Vec<u8>` actions) | See "Why not type-erased" above. |
   178	| Trait object dispatch over `Box<dyn Action>` | UniFFI doesn't support trait objects across FFI cleanly. Same root issue. |
   179	| Cargo features on a monolithic crate | Explosive feature matrix; binding regeneration on every feature combination; doesn't help with app-specific types. |
   180	| Procedural macros generating per-module FFI | Tried in head. Inverts the dependency direction — modules would need to know about FFI exposure. The codegen tool generating an aggregating crate is cleaner. |
