 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# Applesauce — Signers
     2	
     3	> Source: `/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce` @ `da5ec22b`.
     4	
     5	## 1. The `ISigner` contract
     6	
     7	`packages/signers/src/interop.ts:5-16`:
     8	
     9	```ts
    10	export type ISigner = {
    11	  getPublicKey: () => Promise<string>;
    12	  signEvent: (template: EventTemplate) => Promise<NostrEvent>;
    13	  nip04?: { encrypt(pk, pt): Promise<string>; decrypt(pk, ct): Promise<string> };
    14	  nip44?: { encrypt(pk, pt): Promise<string>; decrypt(pk, ct): Promise<string> };
    15	};
    16	```
    17	
    18	The four methods are the entire contract. `nip04` and `nip44` are optional and signer-dependent — readonly omits everything beyond `getPublicKey`, ExtensionSigner proxies to whatever the extension implements.
    19	
    20	## 2. The eight signer implementations
    21	
    22	| Signer | LOC | File | Notes |
    23	| --- | ---: | --- | --- |
    24	| `PrivateKeySigner` | 38 | `signers/private-key-signer.ts` | Plain in-memory key. NIP-04 + NIP-44 via nostr-tools. |
    25	| `PasswordSigner` | 126 | `signers/password-signer.ts` | NIP-49 ncryptsec, locked-by-default; emits a deferred unlock promise. |
    26	| `ReadonlySigner` | 56 | `signers/readonly-signer.ts` | Pubkey-only. All sign/encrypt throw. |
    27	| `ExtensionSigner` | 36 | `signers/extension-signer.ts` | NIP-07 `window.nostr` proxy. Caches pubkey on first call. |
    28	| `NostrConnectSigner` | 464 | `signers/nostr-connect-signer.ts` | NIP-46. Most complex by far. |
    29	| `NostrConnectProvider` | 571 | `signers/nostr-connect-provider.ts` | The remote (signer-side) of NIP-46. |
    30	| `AmberClipboardSigner` | 174 | `signers/amber-clipboard-signer.ts` | Android Amber via intent URIs + clipboard. |
    31	| `AndroidNativeSigner` | 121 | `signers/android-native-signer.ts` | Capacitor plugin. |
    32	| `SerialPortSigner` | 294 | `signers/serial-port-signer.ts` | Hardware signer over WebSerial. |
    33	| `SimpleSigner` | 6 | `signers/simple-signer.ts` | Deprecated alias for PrivateKeySigner. |
    34	
    35	For NMP, the relevant analogues are PrivateKey (in-memory dev), Password (encrypted at rest on disk/keychain), Readonly (npub login), Extension (web only — wasm), NostrConnect (NIP-46, all platforms), and platform-native (iOS Secure Enclave, Android Keystore — Applesauce doesn't have direct analogues but the contract shape is right).
    36	
    37	## 3. `PrivateKeySigner` — the reference (`private-key-signer.ts`)
    38	
    39	38 lines. Holds `Uint8Array` key, derives pubkey on demand, signs via nostr-tools `finalizeEvent`, encrypts via `nip04`/`nip44.v2`. `fromKey(privateKey: Uint8Array | string)` normalizes hex / nsec via `normalizeToSecretKey` (line 33-37). Nothing surprising — this is the shape every other signer should reduce to.
    40	
    41	## 4. `PasswordSigner` — locked-by-default with deferred unlock (`password-signer.ts`)
    42	
    43	Key idea: the signer can be created without a key (just an ncryptsec + lock state). Any call to a privileged method (`getPublicKey`, `signEvent`, encrypt/decrypt) routes through `requestUnlock()`:
    44	
    45	```ts
    46	// :39-45
    47	protected requestUnlock() {
    48	  if (this.key) return;
    49	  if (this.unlockPromise) return this.unlockPromise;
    50	  const p = createDefer<void>();
    51	  this.unlockPromise = p;
    52	  return p;
    53	}
    54	```
    55	
    56	`requestUnlock` returns the **same** deferred promise across all callers — so 50 simultaneous signing attempts block on one unlock prompt. The application UI subscribes (somehow — not in this file) to know "I need to ask the user for a password." The unlock side calls `unlock(password)` (lines 63-74) which decrypts and assigns `this.key`. There's no explicit resolution of `unlockPromise` in the code path — it's broken by the fact that `key` is now set, so the next time the queued operation re-checks it can proceed. **This is subtle.** See gotchas §G7.
    57	
    58	`lock()` sets `key = null` but does not clear `unlockPromise`. Lock-then-unlock-then-sign should work but is not explicitly tested in the file.
    59	
    60	`testPassword(password)` (lines 55-60) is the "verify without committing" path — useful for UX where you want to confirm the password before mutating state.
    61	
    62	## 5. `ExtensionSigner` — pubkey caching (`extension-signer.ts`)
    63	
    64	36 lines. Two important details:
    65	
    66	- Pubkey is **cached after first successful `getPublicKey()`** (lines 17, 22-28). Commit `0867a502` added this; without it, every action triggered a window.nostr.getPublicKey() round-trip.
    67	- `nip04`/`nip44` are getters that return `window.nostr?.nip04` directly (lines 10-15). If the extension lacks the namespace, `signer.nip04` is undefined — callers must check. **No proxy-with-error.** This means `account.nip04?.encrypt(...)` is the safe shape.
    68	- `signEvent` verifies the returned event (line 32-33) — defensive against buggy extensions. NMP's signer wrapper should do the same.
    69	
    70	The `ExtensionMissingError` class (line 6-7) is a discrete error type so UI can render "install nostr extension" CTAs without string-matching.
    71	
    72	## 6. `NostrConnectSigner` — NIP-46 client (`nostr-connect-signer.ts`)
    73	
    74	The complex one. 464 lines. Architecture:
    75	
    76	- Holds a **local** `PrivateKeySigner` for client-side encryption to the remote (line 39, 122).
    77	- Owns a `req: Subscription` (line 135) that opens a long-lived `subscriptionMethod(relays, [{kinds:[24133], '#p':[clientPubkey]}])` (lines 145-161). Uses `repeat()` + `retry()` to survive disconnects — see commit `e6d5613b` ("Fix nostr connect signers not reconnecting to relays").
    78	- `requests: Map<id, Deferred>` (line 186) and `auths: Set<id>` (line 187) track pending RPCs.
    79	- `handleEvent` (lines 190-239):
    80	  - Verifies signature.
    81	  - Drops events not from the configured remote (line 194) — this is how `connect()` is symmetric: the first event with `result === "ack"` (or the supplied secret) sets `remote = event.pubkey` (lines 207-214).
    82	  - Decrypts content using whichever scheme the content looks like (`isNIP04` heuristic, line 199-201). Tries hidden-content cache first (line 198) for already-decrypted gift-wrap-style content.
    83	  - Routes `auth_url` responses to `onAuth(url)` (lines 220-232) — the application supplies a popup-opener; the signer dedups via `auths` set so the popup opens once per request.
    84	- `makeRequest(method, params, kind?)` (lines 252-278) generates a nanoid, encrypts with NIP-44, signs, publishes, awaits the deferred. Handles both `Promise` and `Observable` return shapes from `publishMethod` (lines 271-273).
    85	- `waitForSigner(abort?)` (lines 306-322) — returns a promise that resolves on first ack from a remote. Supports AbortSignal (commit `c290264d`). `close()` cancels it (commit `29d53501`).
    86	- `switchRelays()` (lines 413-430) — NIP-46 extension to ask the remote what relays to migrate to; if non-null, tears down the subscription and rebuilds with the new relays. Commit `0cdd0edd`.
    87	- `fromBunkerURI(uri, opts)` (lines 453-463) — parses `bunker://`, constructs the client, connects with the supplied secret/permissions.
    88	
    89	Notable design choices:
    90	
    91	- The signer **owns the relay connection**, configured via `subscriptionMethod` / `publishMethod` / `pool` (interop.ts:32-81). Static fallbacks on the class (lines 52-56) let an app set globally. NMP should mirror this: signer-owned connection but with explicit dependency injection (no globals).
    92	- Static `buildSigningPermissions(kinds)` (line 448) and `parseBunkerURI` (line 443) make discoverability easy.
    93	
    94	## 7. `AmberClipboardSigner` — the gnarliest UX path (`amber-clipboard-signer.ts`)
    95	
    96	This is the **what-not-to-do reference** as much as a reference implementation. The clipboard is the IPC channel:
    97	
    98	- `intentRequest(intent)` (lines 60-71): opens the intent URI in a new window, then awaits a `pendingRequest: Deferred<string>` that gets resolved by a `visibilitychange` listener reading the clipboard 200ms after the page becomes visible again (lines 46-58).
    99	- Race: the `visibilitychange` fires immediately on `window.open`, before the user has actually responded. Guarded with a 500ms `setTimeout` before installing the pending request (lines 65-68). See gotchas §G8.
   100	- `rejectPending()` cancels any in-flight request — needed because the user could initiate a second signing before completing the first.
   101	- All signing operations construct an `intent:...#Intent;scheme=nostrsigner;...end` URI with payload JSON-URL-encoded (lines 145-173).
   102	
   103	For NMP iOS/macOS, the analogous path is the URL-scheme deeplink to nsec.app or a hardware signer; the Amber implementation is the closest mental model.
   104	
   105	## 8. `AndroidNativeSigner` — Capacitor plugin proxy (`android-native-signer.ts`)
   106	
   107	121 lines. Holds a `packageName` (the installed signer app id), a permissions list, cached pubkey. All methods delegate to `NostrSignerPlugin.<method>(packageName, payload, nanoid(), ...)` (lines 99-119). `setup()` (lines 49-61) calls `setPackageName` and `getPublicKey` once.
   108	
   109	`signEvent` (lines 67-96) is the one with substance: it pre-computes the event hash via `getEventHash`, passes `id` to the plugin (which the plugin uses for request correlation), then verifies the returned signature. The pubkey is set on the template before hashing (line 71-74) — critical, since the plugin doesn't add it.
   110	
   111	## 9. The account layer adds three things on top
   112	
   113	`packages/accounts/src/account.ts` (187 lines) wraps `ISigner` with:
   114	
   115	1. **Per-account request queue** (lines 137-187). `operation()` routes every signer call through `waitForQueue()` which chains promises. Reasons:
   116	   - Some signers (Extension, NostrConnect) cannot handle concurrent requests reliably.
   117	   - User-facing prompts (Password unlock, Amber intent) need serialization.
   118	   - The queue is opt-out via `disableQueue` (lines 49, 150).
   119	   - `abortQueue(reason)` cancels everything in-flight (lines 131-133, 138).
   120	
   121	2. **`SignerMismatchError` post-conditions** (lines 33, 109, 123-124). After `getPublicKey`, asserts result matches account's stored pubkey. After `signEvent`, asserts `result.pubkey === this.pubkey` AND `result.id === precomputed id`. This **catches a signer modifying the event before signing**, e.g., a malicious extension changing kind. NMP **must** copy this.
   122	
   123	3. **`metadata$` BehaviorSubject** (lines 51-57) for app-level per-account state (name, color, etc.) that survives serialization.
   124	
   125	`packages/accounts/src/proxy-signer.ts` (36 lines, not read but inferred) provides a stable `ISigner` that proxies to whichever account is currently active in `AccountManager.active$` (`manager.ts:21-28`). This is what makes `new EventFactory({ signer: manager.signer })` work without re-binding when the active account changes.
   126	
   127	## 10. `AccountManager` — serialization, swapping, queue (`packages/accounts/src/manager.ts`)
   128	
   129	193 lines. Three concerns:
   130	
   131	- **Account-type registry** (lines 33-42): `registerType(AccountClass)` keyed by `static type`. `fromJSON`/`toJSON` per class, manager handles the dispatch in `fromJSON([])` (lines 161-178).
   132	- **Active account** (lines 99-115): `BehaviorSubject<IAccount | undefined>`, with set/clear/get. `removeAccount` clears active if the removed account was active (lines 82-84).
   133	- **Replace** (lines 87-95): add new, swap active if old was active, remove old. This is the account-swap path that powered the bug in commit `96120548` ("Fix ActionRunner cache causing actions to have wrong `user` when accounts change") — see gotchas §G9.
   134	
   135	## 11. NMP design implications
   136	
   137	- The `ISigner` shape is right. Don't expand it. Optional `nip04`/`nip44` is the correct shape — different signers genuinely have different capability sets.
   138	- Bake `SignerMismatchError`'s post-conditions into NMP's signer wrapper. They are cheap and catch a real class of attacks.
   139	- The per-account queue is **load-bearing**, not an optimization. Without it, two concurrent React renders that both ask for `getPublicKey()` from a password-locked signer will trigger two unlock prompts.
   140	- For NIP-46, copy the `repeat() + retry()` subscription pattern (commit `e6d5613b`). A bunker that drops a websocket should not silently kill the signer.
   141	- For the Apple ecosystem specifically: model the platform-native signer (Secure Enclave / hardware key) after `AndroidNativeSigner` — proxy, cache pubkey, pre-compute id, verify signature.
   142	- `NostrConnectSigner.switchRelays` is a real NIP-46 capability that NMP should support to allow bunkers to redirect clients to better relays without re-pairing.
   143	- **The `bunker://` URI parser + connector is one static method (`parseBunkerURI` + `fromBunkerURI`)** — NMP framework-magic must expose this same one-call onboarding as `Signer.fromBunkerUri(url) -> ImpersonatedSigner`.

/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/packages/common/src/helpers/comment.ts:1:import { getOrComputeCachedValue } from "applesauce-core/helpers/cache";
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:7:Event caching is designed to work alongside the synchronous [`EventStore`](https://applesauce.build/typedoc/classes/applesauce-core.EventStore.html). It's generally implemented using the [`persistEventsToCache`](https://applesauce.build/typedoc/functions/applesauce-core.Helpers.persistEventsToCache.html) helper method and a `cacheRequest` function that plugs into event loaders.
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:11:Event caching is designed to work alongside the synchronous `EventStore` so that web applications can take advantage of the in-memory nature of the sync event store. The event store provides fast, synchronous access to events in memory, while the cache provides persistent storage for frequently accessed events like profiles, contacts, and other replaceable events. Unlike event databases, which persist all events, event caching allows you to selectively cache only the events you need.
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:33:Event caching allows you to cache only the events you need, typically:
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:38:- **Specific event IDs** - Events you want to cache for offline access
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:44:1. **`persistEventsToCache`** - Non-blocking: automatically saves new events to the cache **after** they are added to the store
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:45:2. **`cacheRequest`** - Loads events from the cache when requested by event loaders, so cache operations don't block the event store
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:62:// Create a cache (e.g., using nostr-idb)
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:66:// Create a cache request function
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:67:const cacheRequest = (filters) => nostrIDB.filters(filters);
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:69:// Automatically persist new events to the cache
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:78:// Use the cache with event loaders
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:80:  cacheRequest,
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:89:1. **Event Loader Checks Cache First**: When an event loader needs to load an event, it first checks the cache using `cacheRequest`. This happens outside the event store, so it doesn't block synchronous operations.
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:90:2. **Cache Miss Falls Back to Relays**: If the event isn't in the cache, the loader fetches it from relays and adds it to the event store.
/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/index.md:91:3. **New Events Are Cached Asynchronously**: When new events are added to the event store, they're immediately available in memory. `persistEventsToCache` then saves them to the cache **after** they're added (non-blocking), so the event store remains fast and synchronous.

/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/storage/caching/nostr-idb.md:7:The `nostr-idb` package provides a browser-based IndexedDB storage solution for Nostr events, allowing you to cache events locally in web applications built with Applesauce.

/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce/apps/docs/core/event-memory.md:75:When events are duplicated, cached data becomes fragmented:

/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba /Users/pablofernandez/Work/NDK-nhlteu/react/src/session/store/switch-to-user.ts | sed -n '1,90p'" in /Users/pablofernandez/Work/nostr-multi-platform
 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	import type { Hexpubkey, NDKSigner } from "@nostr-dev-kit/ndk";
     2	import { useNDKMutes } from "../../mutes/store";
     3	import { useNDKStore } from "../../ndk/store";
     4	import type { NDKSessionsState } from "./types";
     5	
     6	/**
     7	 * Implementation for switching the active user session.
     8	 * Sets the activePubkey in the store and synchronizes with the mute store.
     9	 *
    10	 * @note This function should not be called directly. Use the useNDKSessionSwitch hook instead.
    11	 * @see useNDKSessionSwitch in src/session/hooks/index.ts
    12	 */
    13	export const switchToUser = (
    14	    set: (partial: Partial<NDKSessionsState> | ((state: NDKSessionsState) => Partial<NDKSessionsState>)) => void,
    15	    get: () => NDKSessionsState,
    16	    pubkey: Hexpubkey | null,
    17	): void => {
    18	    const signers = get().signers;
    19	    const ndk = get().ndk;
    20	    if (!ndk) {
    21	        console.error("Cannot switch user: NDK instance not initialized in session store.");
    22	        return;
    23	    }
    24	
    25	    let signer: NDKSigner | undefined;
    26	
    27	    if (pubkey !== null) {
    28	        // Activate user
    29	        const session = get().sessions.get(pubkey);
    30	
    31	        if (!session) {
    32	            console.error(`Cannot switch to user ${pubkey}: Session does not exist.`);
    33	            return;
    34	        }
    35	
    36	        signer = signers.get(pubkey);
    37	    }
    38	
    39	    // Set NDK signer (can be undefined if no signer is associated with the session)
    40	    useNDKStore.getState().setSigner(signer);
    41	
    42	    // Update active pubkey and lastActive timestamp immutably
    43	    set((state) => {
    44	        let newSessions = state.sessions;
    45	        const newActivePubkey: Hexpubkey | undefined = pubkey === null ? undefined : pubkey;
    46	        if (pubkey) {
    47	            const session = state.sessions.get(pubkey);
    48	            if (session) {
    49	                const updatedSession = { ...session, lastActive: Date.now() / 1000 };
    50	                newSessions = new Map(state.sessions);
    51	                newSessions.set(pubkey, updatedSession);
    52	            }
    53	        }
    54	        return {
    55	            activePubkey: newActivePubkey,
    56	            sessions: newSessions,
    57	        };
    58	    });
    59	
    60	    // Synchronize with mute store
    61	    const muteStore = useNDKMutes.getState();
    62	
    63	    // Update the active pubkey in the mute store
    64	    muteStore.setActivePubkey(pubkey);
    65	
    66	    // Initialize mutes for the active user if they exist
    67	    if (pubkey) {
    68	        muteStore.initMutes(pubkey);
    69	    }
    70	};

 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# M8 — Multi-session (multi-account) clients
     2	
     3	> Part of the [Build & Validation Plan](../plan.md). Arc 1 — Kernel substrate + Nostr social stack.
     4	
     5	**Demo product:** Twitter slice gets an account switcher. Logged-in users can add a second account, switch between them, and each account's timeline / contacts / reactions are correctly isolated.
     6	
     7	**Scope.** Per spec doctrine D4 (single writer per fact) extended to account scope:
     8	
     9	**Subsystem deliverables.**
    10	
    11	- Session model in the kernel: `SessionState { accounts, active, status }` with N accounts simultaneously valid.
    12	- View specs that depend on the active account (Timeline of "your follows", DMs inbox, zap history) get account-scoped composite keys.
    13	- Account switch is an action with full rebuild semantics — open views for the new active account, close the prior ones, projection caches stay populated across switches when overlap exists.
    14	- Per-account signer binding (each account has its own `IdentityId`).
    15	- Per-account secure storage namespacing in `KeychainCapability`.
    16	
    17	**Exit gate.**
    18	
    19	- Bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap): two accounts active, switch between them, assert no state bleed. `AppState` snapshot for account A never contains data scoped to account B's session-aware views.
    20	- Switching accounts during an in-flight publish: the publish is account-tagged, completes correctly, lands in the originating account's timeline only.
    21	- Per-account signer never signs an event for the wrong account (test forces dispatch through a wrong-account signer; the action ledger rejects).
    22	
    23	**Runnable artifact.** Account switcher in iOS demo with two real test accounts. Report in `docs/perf/m8/multi-account.md`.

 succeeded in 124ms:
docs/perf/firehose-bench/1779070804-live.md:18:- Scenarios requiring LMDB, NIP-65 outbox, NIP-77, NIP-42 auth, multi-account, NSE, or soak are not implemented for M1.
docs/aim.md:13:The framing concern is one sentence: **make it nearly impossible to build a broken Nostr application.** Today, building a Nostr client involves dozens of subtle correctness pitfalls — stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, wrong relays for wrong events, race conditions between local state and relay state, leaked signing operations, multi-account state desync. The framework's job is to make each of those classes of bug structurally impossible through the safe app-kernel and FFI API: not merely documented as a footgun or caught by a linter, but ruled out by the type system, actor ownership, and public API surface. Lower-level Rust escape hatches, when they exist for internal subsystems or tests, must be explicitly named, instrumented, and covered by regression tests.

SIX-AGENT SURGE dispatched in one tick: T39 M4 NIP-77 negentropy impl (new crate nmp-nip77, reconciler+watermarks+cap negotiation+3 triggers+RunSync+4 integration tests); T40 M5 NIP-42 auth impl (relay state machine + REQ queue/replay + publish retry + 5 tests incl. D8 rev-stability); T41 M11 Step 0 podcast (Swift verbatim copy from /Users/pablofernandez/src/podcast → ios/NmpPodcast/ + scaffold 5 podcast crates nmp-podcast-{core,feeds,audio,rag,llm} + per-crate smoke tests); T42 M11.5 Step 0 (nmp-nip29 full impl + Highlighter scaffold from /Users/pablofernandez/src/hl/app); T43 M6 sessions+signers (Signer trait + Local/Bunker/NIP-07 impls + multi-account IdentityModule + kind:3 auto-rewire + 7 integration tests — UNBLOCKS T40); T44 T43 M10.5 round-4 (kernel/mod.rs HARD-cap split, kernel/ingest.rs HARD-cap split, S3 real-sig 100k, apply_burst false-pass fix, missing G-S4 gates recover_after_stall + monotonic_rev, doctrine D7→D0 cite fix, OnceLock consistency). All worktree-isolated, HEAD:master push refspec, post-merge codex required. T38 (M3 round-4) continues in background. 7 agents in flight total. |
docs/plan.md:41:| [M8](plan/m8-multi-account.md) | Multi-session (multi-account) clients | Arc 1 — Social stack | |
docs/product-spec/overview-and-dx.md:23:The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.
docs/perf/firehose-bench/1779069727-live.md:18:- Scenarios requiring LMDB, NIP-65 outbox, NIP-77, NIP-42 auth, multi-account, NSE, or soak are not implemented for M1.
docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:5:> **Scope:** High-level product and architecture lessons to preserve before NMP's outbox, relay policy, event store, and action layers are built. This is not an implementation plan.
docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:31:NMP should also avoid a monolithic "Nostr client object" that quietly accumulates cache policy, relay policy, subscription grouping, publishing, sessions, and product behavior. The public path can feel simple, but internally the boundaries need to stay sharp.
docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:70:The sync engine should share relay policy with the subscription planner. If live reads for an author go to the author's outbox relays, historical reconciliation for that same view should not quietly use a different relay universe. Sync, live REQ, cache reads, and fallback discovery are different execution modes for the same logical interest.
docs/design/nip29/kinds.md:120:- **Effect:** relay removes the target event from its store and (per relay policy) refuses to redeliver it.

docs/design/ffi-hardening/harness-runners.md:152:- **Multi-account.** S5/S6 use a single account; multi-account
docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:62:    /// Bound to a specific account (multi-account UIs, M8). Re-routes on that
docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:64:    Account(AccountId),
docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:167:The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in [`docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`](../../plan/m8-multi-account.md) — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.

docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md:113:M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine that actually emits it. For M2, the trigger fires once at startup with `from: None, to: Some(active)` so the test surface can exercise account-scope binding without waiting for M8.
docs/design/framework-magic/signers.md:13:In both cases the new account becomes available to the active-session machinery (per C12) on a subsequent `SwitchActiveAccount` dispatch.
docs/design/framework-magic/signers.md:40:   e. Assert a follow-up `SwitchActiveAccount { account_id }` succeeds and that the actor can sign a test event using the newly-created identity (round-trip: dispatch a `SendNote` against the new account, observe a signed event in the action ledger before publish).
docs/design/framework-magic/outbox.md:68:- NIP-77 sync routing — that's C10 in `sync.md`. Sync and live REQ should share relay policy (`ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §6 last paragraph), but the symmetric assertion lives with C10.
docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:12:- `SessionState` (`subsystems.md` §7.4 lines 107–125) carries `accounts: Vec<Account>` and `active: Option<String>` as plain state fields. A `SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey }` action mutates `active`; the mutation is the only state change.
docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:18:**App writes:** one dispatch: `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey })`. The app's "switch account" UI is a button that fires that dispatch. No log-out / log-in dance, no view-tree rebuild, no manual REQ reissue, no clearing of caches — the framework handles all of it as a single tick of the actor's event loop.
docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:26:3. **Dispatch switch:** `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey: bob_pk })`. The test makes no other calls; the harness drains the action ledger and the planner trigger queue.
docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:33:**Milestone owner:** **[PENDING M8]**. M8 is the multi-account session milestone (per `scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md` ladder). M2 already lands the `Trigger::ActiveAccountChanged` shape (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2 line 109: "M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine"). Test checked in as `#[ignore = "pending M8 multi-account state machine"]`. Sub-paths 4 and 7 are testable as soon as M2 lands (single-account boot fires the trigger once with `from: None, to: Some(active)` per the M2 design); the rest needs M8.
docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:37:The eight sub-paths assert different facets of one observable contract: *after the switch dispatch, every consequence is a derived re-emission, never an imperative reissue.* The kernel-substrate (`kernel-substrate.md` §8) ensures domain-store isolation; the planner (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2) ensures interest re-resolution; the identity machinery (`kernel-substrate.md` §6) ensures signer rebinding. The contract bullet covers all three as one because they are observed together: an app that does `dispatch(SwitchActiveAccount)` and then attempts any operation gets a correctly-rebound system; partial rebinding is a regression.
docs/decisions/0008-twitter-clone-on-ios-as-the-slice-target.md:103:- `AppAction::ActivateAccount { pubkey }` action.
docs/decisions/0008-twitter-clone-on-ios-as-the-slice-target.md:168:- **Phase 1b becomes "expand to multi-relay + outbox + full view kinds + other platforms"** rather than "expand to anything beyond Profile." Some Phase 2 (sync engine, negentropy) and Phase 3 (multi-account, more signer kinds) work also reasonably moves earlier.
docs/design/framework-magic/sync.md:58:- The "shared relay policy between sync and live REQ" lesson from `ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §6 last paragraph is implicit in the per-relay watermark — both engines key by the same `(filter_sig, relay_url)` pair, so they cannot disagree on the relay universe.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/c077825.md:390:    23	The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.
docs/research/sessions/ndk-sessions.md:3:> Source: `/Users/pablofernandez/Work/NDK-nhlteu` (master). Read for NMP M6 to learn what the most-deployed JS Nostr library actually does about multi-account, signer plumbing, persistence boundaries, and reactive switching.
docs/research/sessions/ndk-sessions.md:49:**Applies to NMP:** the persistence boundary is principled — persist *identity + signer descriptor + user preferences*; everything else is derived from the event store at next start. This maps cleanly onto NMP's actor-owned domain stores: the kernel persists `AccountId` + `IdentityDescriptor` blob; follow set, mute set, relay list are derived from the verified event store.
docs/research/sessions/ndk-sessions.md:69:**Diverges from NMP:** NDK's manager owns NDK-instance side effects directly (`ndk.signer = …`, `ndk.muteFilter = …`). NMP's signer is selected by `ActivateAccount { pubkey }` (`docs/product-spec/api-surface.md:108`) and bound through the actor; no public assignment of a global "current signer" exists.
docs/research/sessions/ndk-sessions.md:96:The signer is wired *before* `activePubkey` flips so any zustand subscriber waking from the activePubkey change sees a fully-configured NDK. **Applies to NMP:** the actor's `ActivateAccount` handler must publish a single `AppUpdate::FullState` after *all* derived state is rebuilt — partial-update visibility is a class of bug NDK hit (the comment is "//prevents race conditions").
docs/research/sessions/ndk-sessions.md:100:`store.ts:286-316` — `ndk.muteFilter` becomes a closure over the *session's* mute data; `ndk.relayConnectionFilter` becomes a closure over `blockedRelays`. Both are cleared on switch-to-null. **Applies to NMP:** the kernel's per-account filter sets must be rebound atomically with `ActivateAccount`; nothing outside the actor should hold a "current mute filter" reference.
docs/research/sessions/ndk-sessions.md:274:| Global `ndk.signer = …` mutation | `store.ts:282` | **skip** — NMP uses `ActivateAccount` intent |
docs/design/framework-magic/test-scaffolding.md:199:| M8 | C12 (multi-account state machine) |
docs/design/framework-magic/kind3.md:15:2. Emits an internal planner trigger — proposed name `Trigger::FollowListChanged { account: AccountId, prev_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey>, next_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey> }` — symmetric to the existing `Trigger::Nip65Arrived` (`docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.1).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:89:+- **M4 (NIP-77 negentropy sync), M5 (NIP-42 auth), M6 (sessions + signers + write), M7 (interaction loop), M8 (multi-session):** designs pending after M2/M3 hardens.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:480:+2. Emits an internal planner trigger — proposed name `Trigger::FollowListChanged { account: AccountId, prev_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey>, next_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey> }` — symmetric to the existing `Trigger::Nip65Arrived` (`docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.1).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:608:+- NIP-77 sync routing — that's C10 in `sync.md`. Sync and live REQ should share relay policy (`ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §6 last paragraph), but the symmetric assertion lives with C10.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:725:+- `SessionState` (`subsystems.md` §7.4 lines 107–125) carries `accounts: Vec<Account>` and `active: Option<String>` as plain state fields. A `SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey }` action mutates `active`; the mutation is the only state change.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:731:+**App writes:** one dispatch: `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey })`. The app's "switch account" UI is a button that fires that dispatch. No log-out / log-in dance, no view-tree rebuild, no manual REQ reissue, no clearing of caches — the framework handles all of it as a single tick of the actor's event loop.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:739:+3. **Dispatch switch:** `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey: bob_pk })`. The test makes no other calls; the harness drains the action ledger and the planner trigger queue.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:746:+**Milestone owner:** **[PENDING M8]**. M8 is the multi-account session milestone (per `scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md` ladder). M2 already lands the `Trigger::ActiveAccountChanged` shape (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2 line 109: "M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine"). Test checked in as `#[ignore = "pending M8 multi-account state machine"]`. Sub-paths 4 and 7 are testable as soon as M2 lands (single-account boot fires the trigger once with `from: None, to: Some(active)` per the M2 design); the rest needs M8.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:750:+The eight sub-paths assert different facets of one observable contract: *after the switch dispatch, every consequence is a derived re-emission, never an imperative reissue.* The kernel-substrate (`kernel-substrate.md` §8) ensures domain-store isolation; the planner (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2) ensures interest re-resolution; the identity machinery (`kernel-substrate.md` §6) ensures signer rebinding. The contract bullet covers all three as one because they are observed together: an app that does `dispatch(SwitchActiveAccount)` and then attempts any operation gets a correctly-rebound system; partial rebinding is a regression.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:795:+In both cases the new account becomes available to the active-session machinery (per C12) on a subsequent `SwitchActiveAccount` dispatch.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:822:+   e. Assert a follow-up `SwitchActiveAccount { account_id }` succeeds and that the actor can sign a test event using the newly-created identity (round-trip: dispatch a `SendNote` against the new account, observe a signed event in the action ledger before publish).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:976:+- The "shared relay policy between sync and live REQ" lesson from `ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §6 last paragraph is implicit in the per-relay watermark — both engines key by the same `(filter_sig, relay_url)` pair, so they cannot disagree on the relay universe.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:3702:    23	The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/51120cb.md:3936:    58	- The "shared relay policy between sync and live REQ" lesson from `ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §6 last paragraph is implicit in the per-relay watermark — both engines key by the same `(filter_sig, relay_url)` pair, so they cannot disagree on the relay universe.

Emits an internal planner trigger — proposed name `Trigger::FollowListChanged { account: AccountId, prev_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey>, next_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey> }` — symmetric to the existing `Trigger::Nip65Arrived` (`docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.1).
docs/plan/status.md:23:- Multi-account / multi-session model and account switching.
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:45:    pub id: AccountId,           // ULID; distinct from pubkey (supports multiple accounts for one pubkey)
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:56:    pub id: AccountId,
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:63:`AccountId` (ULID) distinct from `pubkey` — required because applesauce allows multiple accounts for one pubkey (`manager.ts:60-63`), and NMP must support "same nsec, different relay policy" or "same bunker user from two devices."
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:78:ActivateAccount { pubkey: String },
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:94:**Switch order invariant** (from NDK `store.ts:279-329`): on `ActivateAccount`, the actor must (1) bind the signer, (2) rebuild mute/relay filters, (3) update active_pubkey, (4) emit ONE `AppUpdate::FullState`. Never partial updates.
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:102:Per-view reactivity (e.g., a SessionSwitcher view) reads `AppState.session.accounts` and `AppState.session.active_pubkey`; re-renders when those change. The framework handles the diff; the view code is declarative.
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:114:| All received events (kind:0, 3, 10000, 10001, 10002, etc.) | Encrypted `AccountRecord` per account (one keyring entry per `AccountId`) | `ActiveAccount` (derived on activate) |
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:120:LMDB stores the *index* — `AccountId → keyring_key` — plus the public account record (display name, namespace, pubkey, last_active, created_at). The keyring stores the secret material.
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:130:**NMP's preferred mechanism**: a kernel-owned **account-bootstrap planner** that, on `ActivateAccount`, opens a fixed subscription `{kinds: [0, 3, 10000, 10001, 10002], authors: [pubkey]}` against the user's outbox relays (or fallback). Per-kind event handlers update the corresponding domain store (`ProfileStore`, `FollowSetStore`, `MuteSetStore`, `RelayListStore`). The subscription is owned by the account session and torn down on `RemoveAccount`/`ActivateAccount(other)`.
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:183:| Generate new nsec + NIP-49 encrypt + keyring store | Multi-account switcher UI (M8 per `docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`) |
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:235:- `crates/nmp-core/src/app/state.rs` — add `session: SessionState { accounts: Vec<AccountRecord>, active_pubkey: Option<Hexpubkey> }`.
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:236:- `crates/nmp-core/src/actions/account.rs` (new) — handlers for `AddAccount*`, `ActivateAccount`, `RemoveAccount`, `GenerateNewAccount`.
docs/research/sessions/synthesis.md:243:NMP's actor model gives this for free: one `AccountId` is the unit of everything. The "session" is just "this account is currently active" — a single `Option<AccountId>` in `AppState`. Derived state lives in domain stores keyed by pubkey, queryable for any account whether active or not.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/bc84cfe.md:7773:    23	The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/bc84cfe.md:7937:    18	- Scenarios requiring LMDB, NIP-65 outbox, NIP-77, NIP-42 auth, multi-account, NSE, or soak are not implemented for M1.
docs/design/app-extension-kernel.md:204:    HumanAccount(AccountId),
docs/research/sessions/applesauce-sessions.md:3:> Source: `/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce`. Applesauce uses the term **"accounts"** for what NDK calls "sessions"; the multi-account container is `AccountManager`. The wire-level signer abstraction is `ISigner`. See `docs/research/applesauce/signers.md` (already in repo) for the signer-only deep-dive; this doc covers the account/session layer + delta against the existing signer doc.
docs/research/sessions/applesauce-sessions.md:132:**Applies to NMP:** rxjs maps mechanically to NMP's `AppUpdate` stream. The de-dup discipline matters: the actor should not emit a `FullState` if `active_pubkey` didn't actually change (`AppUpdate` deltas already cover this if implemented carefully).
docs/design/podcast/podcast-rag.md:68:Per-app SQLite DB file at `<data_dir>/podcast/vectors.sqlite`. Single file per device, not per-account — the M11 reference Swift app has no multi-account model. Multi-account is post-M11 (M8 in the NMP plan); the schema is forward-compatible (one row per `(account_id, chunk_id)` becomes the composite key in a v2 migration).
docs/plan/parallelization.md:9:- **[M6](m6-signers-write.md) (signer + write path) is a serialization point** — most downstream milestones ([M7](m7-interaction-loop.md), [M8](m8-multi-account.md), [M9](m9-messaging.md), [M10](m10-blossom.md), [M12](m12-wallet.md)) depend on it. Land this fast.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/d660735.md:823:d660735:docs/decisions/0008-twitter-clone-on-ios-as-the-slice-target.md:168:- **Phase 1b becomes "expand to multi-relay + outbox + full view kinds + other platforms"** rather than "expand to anything beyond Profile." Some Phase 2 (sync engine, negentropy) and Phase 3 (multi-account, more signer kinds) work also reasonably moves earlier.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/d660735.md:869:d660735:docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:5:> **Scope:** High-level product and architecture lessons to preserve before NMP's outbox, relay policy, event store, and action layers are built. This is not an implementation plan.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/d660735.md:875:d660735:docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:70:The sync engine should share relay policy with the subscription planner. If live reads for an author go to the author's outbox relays, historical reconciliation for that same view should not quietly use a different relay universe. Sync, live REQ, cache reads, and fallback discovery are different execution modes for the same logical interest.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/ab632c1.md:62: docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md                      |  23 +
docs/perf/codex-reviews/ab632c1.md:225:-The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in `docs/plan.md` line 284 — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/ab632c1.md:226:+The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in [`docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`](../../plan/m8-multi-account.md) — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/ab632c1.md:2315:docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md:12:- View specs that depend on the active account (Timeline of "your follows", DMs inbox, zap history) get account-scoped composite keys.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/ab632c1.md:2376:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:33:**Milestone owner:** **[PENDING M8]**. M8 is the multi-account session milestone (per `scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md` ladder). M2 already lands the `Trigger::ActiveAccountChanged` shape (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2 line 109: "M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine"). Test checked in as `#[ignore = "pending M8 multi-account state machine"]`. Sub-paths 4 and 7 are testable as soon as M2 lands (single-account boot fires the trigger once with `from: None, to: Some(active)` per the M2 design); the rest needs M8.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/ab632c1.md: WARNING: stopped searching binary file after match (found "\0" byte around offset 256048)
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:1888:docs/aim.md:13:The framing concern is one sentence: **make it nearly impossible to build a broken Nostr application.** Today, building a Nostr client involves dozens of subtle correctness pitfalls — stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, wrong relays for wrong events, race conditions between local state and relay state, leaked signing operations, multi-account state desync. The framework's job is to make each of those classes of bug structurally impossible through the safe app-kernel and FFI API: not merely documented as a footgun or caught by a linter, but ruled out by the type system, actor ownership, and public API surface. Lower-level Rust escape hatches, when they exist for internal subsystems or tests, must be explicitly named, instrumented, and covered by regression tests.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:2059:docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md:7:**Scope.** Per spec doctrine D4 (single writer per fact) extended to account scope:
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:2095:docs/product-spec/overview-and-dx.md:23:The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:2146:docs/perf/codex-reviews/c077825.md:390:    23	The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:2959:docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:2292:docs/aim.md:13:The framing concern is one sentence: **make it nearly impossible to build a broken Nostr application.** Today, building a Nostr client involves dozens of subtle correctness pitfalls — stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, wrong relays for wrong events, race conditions between local state and relay state, leaked signing operations, multi-account state desync. The framework's job is to make each of those classes of bug structurally impossible through the safe app-kernel and FFI API: not merely documented as a footgun or caught by a linter, but ruled out by the type system, actor ownership, and public API surface. Lower-level Rust escape hatches, when they exist for internal subsystems or tests, must be explicitly named, instrumented, and covered by regression tests.

The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:5236:    62	    /// Bound to a specific account (multi-account UIs, M8). Re-routes on that
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:5238:    64	    Account(AccountId),
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:5341:   167	The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in [`docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`](../../plan/m8-multi-account.md) — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/92b8260.md:8278:docs/product-spec/overview-and-dx.md:23:The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. docs/perf/codex-reviews/fb139ab.md:1716:+docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:119:The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in `docs/plan.md` line 284 — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/fb139ab.md:1747:+docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:5:> **Scope:** High-level product and architecture lessons to preserve before NMP's outbox, relay policy, event store, and action layers are built. This is not an implementation plan.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/fb139ab.md:1754:+docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:70:The sync engine should share relay policy with the subscription planner. If live reads for an author go to the author's outbox relays, historical reconciliation for that same view should not quietly use a different relay universe. Sync, live REQ, cache reads, and fallback discovery are different execution modes for the same logical interest.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/0dfb975.md:2161:+Per-app SQLite DB file at `<data_dir>/podcast/vectors.sqlite`. Single file per device, not per-account — the M11 reference Swift app has no multi-account model. Multi-account is post-M11 (M8 in the NMP plan); the schema is forward-compatible (one row per `(account_id, chunk_id)` becomes the composite key in a v2 migration).

docs/perf/codex-reviews/8660961.md:5795:    23	The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:157:++- **M4 (NIP-77 negentropy sync), M5 (NIP-42 auth), M6 (sessions + signers + write), M7 (interaction loop), M8 (multi-session):** designs pending after M2/M3 hardens.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:548:++2. Emits an internal planner trigger — proposed name `Trigger::FollowListChanged { account: AccountId, prev_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey>, next_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey> }` — symmetric to the existing `Trigger::Nip65Arrived` (`docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.1).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:676:++- NIP-77 sync routing — that's C10 in `sync.md`. Sync and live REQ should share relay policy (`ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §6 last paragraph), but the symmetric assertion lives with C10.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:793:++- `SessionState` (`subsystems.md` §7.4 lines 107–125) carries `accounts: Vec<Account>` and `active: Option<String>` as plain state fields. A `SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey }` action mutates `active`; the mutation is the only state change.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:799:++**App writes:** one dispatch: `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey })`. The app's "switch account" UI is a button that fires that dispatch. No log-out / log-in dance, no view-tree rebuild, no manual REQ reissue, no clearing of caches — the framework handles all of it as a single tick of the actor's event loop.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:807:++3. **Dispatch switch:** `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey: bob_pk })`. The test makes no other calls; the harness drains the action ledger and the planner trigger queue.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:814:++**Milestone owner:** **[PENDING M8]**. M8 is the multi-account session milestone (per `scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md` ladder). M2 already lands the `Trigger::ActiveAccountChanged` shape (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2 line 109: "M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine"). Test checked in as `#[ignore = "pending M8 multi-account state machine"]`. Sub-paths 4 and 7 are testable as soon as M2 lands (single-account boot fires the trigger once with `from: None, to: Some(active)` per the M2 design); the rest needs M8.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:818:++The eight sub-paths assert different facets of one observable contract: *after the switch dispatch, every consequence is a derived re-emission, never an imperative reissue.* The kernel-substrate (`kernel-substrate.md` §8) ensures domain-store isolation; the planner (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2) ensures interest re-resolution; the identity machinery (`kernel-substrate.md` §6) ensures signer rebinding. The contract bullet covers all three as one because they are observed together: an app that does `dispatch(SwitchActiveAccount)` and then attempts any operation gets a correctly-rebound system; partial rebinding is a regression.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:863:++In both cases the new account becomes available to the active-session machinery (per C12) on a subsequent `SwitchActiveAccount` dispatch.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:890:++   e. Assert a follow-up `SwitchActiveAccount { account_id }` succeeds and that the actor can sign a test event using the newly-created identity (round-trip: dispatch a `SendNote` against the new account, observe a signed event in the action ledger before publish).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:1044:++- The "shared relay policy between sync and live REQ" lesson from `ndk-applesauce-lessons.md` §6 last paragraph is implicit in the per-relay watermark — both engines key by the same `(filter_sig, relay_url)` pair, so they cannot disagree on the relay universe.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:1257:++| M8 | C12 (multi-account state machine) |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:3313:+docs/design/framework-magic/kind3.md:15:2. Emits an internal planner trigger — proposed name `Trigger::FollowListChanged { account: AccountId, prev_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey>, next_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey> }` — symmetric to the existing `Trigger::Nip65Arrived` (`docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.1).

The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/3f5a2b3.md:6021:    13	The framing concern is one sentence: **make it nearly impossible to build a broken Nostr application.** Today, building a Nostr client involves dozens of subtle correctness pitfalls — stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, wrong relays for wrong events, race conditions between local state and relay state, leaked signing operations, multi-account state desync. The framework's job is to make each of those classes of bug structurally impossible through the safe app-kernel and FFI API: not merely documented as a footgun or caught by a linter, but ruled out by the type system, actor ownership, and public API surface. Lower-level Rust escape hatches, when they exist for internal subsystems or tests, must be explicitly named, instrumented, and covered by regression tests.

M8 is the multi-account session milestone (per `scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md` ladder). docs/perf/codex-reviews/9eace68.md:1704:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:37:The eight sub-paths assert different facets of one observable contract: *after the switch dispatch, every consequence is a derived re-emission, never an imperative reissue.* The kernel-substrate (`kernel-substrate.md` §8) ensures domain-store isolation; the planner (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2) ensures interest re-resolution; the identity machinery (`kernel-substrate.md` §6) ensures signer rebinding. The contract bullet covers all three as one because they are observed together: an app that does `dispatch(SwitchActiveAccount)` and then attempts any operation gets a correctly-rebound system; partial rebinding is a regression.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:5098:   111	    pub active_account:   Option<AccountId>,
docs/perf/codex-reviews/5ff0ac5.md:6531:   119	The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in [`docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`](../../plan/m8-multi-account.md) — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:2041:docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md
docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:2292:docs/aim.md:13:The framing concern is one sentence: **make it nearly impossible to build a broken Nostr application.** Today, building a Nostr client involves dozens of subtle correctness pitfalls — stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, wrong relays for wrong events, race conditions between local state and relay state, leaked signing operations, multi-account state desync. The framework's job is to make each of those classes of bug structurally impossible through the safe app-kernel and FFI API: not merely documented as a footgun or caught by a linter, but ruled out by the type system, actor ownership, and public API surface. Lower-level Rust escape hatches, when they exist for internal subsystems or tests, must be explicitly named, instrumented, and covered by regression tests.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:2354:docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md:111:M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine that actually emits it. For M2, the trigger fires once at startup with `from: None, to: Some(active)` so the test surface can exercise account-scope binding without waiting for M8.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:2381:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:33:**Milestone owner:** **[PENDING M8]**. M8 is the multi-account session milestone (per `scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md` ladder). M2 already lands the `Trigger::ActiveAccountChanged` shape (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2 line 109: "M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine"). Test checked in as `#[ignore = "pending M8 multi-account state machine"]`. Sub-paths 4 and 7 are testable as soon as M2 lands (single-account boot fires the trigger once with `from: None, to: Some(active)` per the M2 design); the rest needs M8.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:2382:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:37:The eight sub-paths assert different facets of one observable contract: *after the switch dispatch, every consequence is a derived re-emission, never an imperative reissue.* The kernel-substrate (`kernel-substrate.md` §8) ensures domain-store isolation; the planner (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2) ensures interest re-resolution; the identity machinery (`kernel-substrate.md` §6) ensures signer rebinding. The contract bullet covers all three as one because they are observed together: an app that does `dispatch(SwitchActiveAccount)` and then attempts any operation gets a correctly-rebound system; partial rebinding is a regression.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/8d633e8.md:2417:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:119:The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in [`docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`](../../plan/m8-multi-account.md) — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.

The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/031fc07.md:1703:docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:5:> **Scope:** High-level product and architecture lessons to preserve before NMP's outbox, relay policy, event store, and action layers are built. This is not an implementation plan.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/031fc07.md:1710:docs/design/ndk-applesauce-lessons.md:70:The sync engine should share relay policy with the subscription planner. If live reads for an author go to the author's outbox relays, historical reconciliation for that same view should not quietly use a different relay universe. Sync, live REQ, cache reads, and fallback discovery are different execution modes for the same logical interest.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/0a10e42.md:3906:docs/perf/codex-reviews/9eace68.md:4873:docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md:7:**Scope.** Per spec doctrine D4 (single writer per fact) extended to account scope:
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:49:+> Source: `/private/tmp/nostr-research/applesauce`. Applesauce uses the term **"accounts"** for what NDK calls "sessions"; the multi-account container is `AccountManager`. The wire-level signer abstraction is `ISigner`. See `docs/research/applesauce/signers.md` (already in repo) for the signer-only deep-dive; this doc covers the account/session layer + delta against the existing signer doc.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:178:+**Applies to NMP:** rxjs maps mechanically to NMP's `AppUpdate` stream. The de-dup discipline matters: the actor should not emit a `FullState` if `active_pubkey` didn't actually change (`AppUpdate` deltas already cover this if implemented carefully).
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:350:+> Source: `/Users/pablofernandez/Work/NDK-nhlteu` (master). Read for NMP M6 to learn what the most-deployed JS Nostr library actually does about multi-account, signer plumbing, persistence boundaries, and reactive switching.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:396:+**Applies to NMP:** the persistence boundary is principled — persist *identity + signer descriptor + user preferences*; everything else is derived from the event store at next start. This maps cleanly onto NMP's actor-owned domain stores: the kernel persists `AccountId` + `IdentityDescriptor` blob; follow set, mute set, relay list are derived from the verified event store.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:416:+**Diverges from NMP:** NDK's manager owns NDK-instance side effects directly (`ndk.signer = …`, `ndk.muteFilter = …`). NMP's signer is selected by `ActivateAccount { pubkey }` (`docs/product-spec/api-surface.md:108`) and bound through the actor; no public assignment of a global "current signer" exists.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:443:+The signer is wired *before* `activePubkey` flips so any zustand subscriber waking from the activePubkey change sees a fully-configured NDK. **Applies to NMP:** the actor's `ActivateAccount` handler must publish a single `AppUpdate::FullState` after *all* derived state is rebuilt — partial-update visibility is a class of bug NDK hit (the comment is "//prevents race conditions").
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:447:+`store.ts:286-316` — `ndk.muteFilter` becomes a closure over the *session's* mute data; `ndk.relayConnectionFilter` becomes a closure over `blockedRelays`. Both are cleared on switch-to-null. **Applies to NMP:** the kernel's per-account filter sets must be rebound atomically with `ActivateAccount`; nothing outside the actor should hold a "current mute filter" reference.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:621:+| Global `ndk.signer = …` mutation | `store.ts:282` | **skip** — NMP uses `ActivateAccount` intent |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:675:+    pub id: AccountId,           // ULID; distinct from pubkey (supports multiple accounts for one pubkey)
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:686:+    pub id: AccountId,
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:693:+`AccountId` (ULID) distinct from `pubkey` — required because applesauce allows multiple accounts for one pubkey (`manager.ts:60-63`), and NMP must support "same nsec, different relay policy" or "same bunker user from two devices."
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:708:+ActivateAccount { pubkey: String },
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:724:+**Switch order invariant** (from NDK `store.ts:279-329`): on `ActivateAccount`, the actor must (1) bind the signer, (2) rebuild mute/relay filters, (3) update active_pubkey, (4) emit ONE `AppUpdate::FullState`. Never partial updates.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:732:+Per-view reactivity (e.g., a SessionSwitcher view) reads `AppState.session.accounts` and `AppState.session.active_pubkey`; re-renders when those change. The framework handles the diff; the view code is declarative.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:744:+| All received events (kind:0, 3, 10000, 10001, 10002, etc.) | Encrypted `AccountRecord` per account (one keyring entry per `AccountId`) | `ActiveAccount` (derived on activate) |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:750:+LMDB stores the *index* — `AccountId → keyring_key` — plus the public account record (display name, namespace, pubkey, last_active, created_at). The keyring stores the secret material.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:760:+**NMP's preferred mechanism**: a kernel-owned **account-bootstrap planner** that, on `ActivateAccount`, opens a fixed subscription `{kinds: [0, 3, 10000, 10001, 10002], authors: [pubkey]}` against the user's outbox relays (or fallback). Per-kind event handlers update the corresponding domain store (`ProfileStore`, `FollowSetStore`, `MuteSetStore`, `RelayListStore`). The subscription is owned by the account session and torn down on `RemoveAccount`/`ActivateAccount(other)`.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:813:+| Generate new nsec + NIP-49 encrypt + keyring store | Multi-account switcher UI (M8 per `docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`) |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:865:+- `crates/nmp-core/src/app/state.rs` — add `session: SessionState { accounts: Vec<AccountRecord>, active_pubkey: Option<Hexpubkey> }`.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:866:+- `crates/nmp-core/src/actions/account.rs` (new) — handlers for `AddAccount*`, `ActivateAccount`, `RemoveAccount`, `GenerateNewAccount`.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:873:+NMP's actor model gives this for free: one `AccountId` is the unit of everything. The "session" is just "this account is currently active" — a single `Option<AccountId>` in `AppState`. Derived state lives in domain stores keyed by pubkey, queryable for any account whether active or not.

The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

D8 rev-stability); T41 M11 Step 0 podcast (Swift verbatim copy from /Users/pablofernandez/src/podcast → ios/NmpPodcast/ + scaffold 5 podcast crates nmp-podcast-{core,feeds,audio,rag,llm} + per-crate smoke tests); T42 M11.5 Step 0 (nmp-nip29 full impl + Highlighter scaffold from /Users/pablofernandez/src/hl/app); T43 M6 sessions+signers (Signer trait + Local/Bunker/NIP-07 impls + multi-account IdentityModule + kind:3 auto-rewire + 7 integration tests — UNBLOCKS T40); T44 T43 M10.5 round-4 (kernel/mod.rs HARD-cap split, kernel/ingest.rs HARD-cap split, S3 real-sig 100k, apply_burst false-pass fix, missing G-S4 gates recover_after_stall + monotonic_rev, doctrine D7→D0 cite fix, OnceLock consistency). docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:1793:docs/aim.md:13:The framing concern is one sentence: **make it nearly impossible to build a broken Nostr application.** Today, building a Nostr client involves dozens of subtle correctness pitfalls — stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, wrong relays for wrong events, race conditions between local state and relay state, leaked signing operations, multi-account state desync. The framework's job is to make each of those classes of bug structurally impossible through the safe app-kernel and FFI API: not merely documented as a footgun or caught by a linter, but ruled out by the type system, actor ownership, and public API surface. Lower-level Rust escape hatches, when they exist for internal subsystems or tests, must be explicitly named, instrumented, and covered by regression tests.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:1815:docs/plan.md:41:| [M8](plan/m8-multi-account.md) | Multi-session (multi-account) clients | Arc 1 — Social stack | |
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:1827:docs/product-spec/overview-and-dx.md:23:The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:2194:docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md:113:M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine that actually emits it. For M2, the trigger fires once at startup with `from: None, to: Some(active)` so the test surface can exercise account-scope binding without waiting for M8.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:2207:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:62:    /// Bound to a specific account (multi-account UIs, M8). Re-routes on that
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:2212:docs/design/subscription-compilation/intro.md:167:The kernel `SessionState` (`docs/product-spec/subsystems.md` §7.4; M8 graduates this to multi-account) carries an active account id. `InterestScope::ActiveAccount` resolves at compile time, not at registration time. On account switch (§4 trigger A4), the compiler re-evaluates every `ActiveAccount`-scoped interest against the new active account's mailbox view. This is the structural enforcement of bug-extinction #5 (account-context overlap) referenced in [`docs/plan/m8-multi-account.md`](../../plan/m8-multi-account.md) — the kernel cannot "forget" to re-route because every plan re-derives from the active scope.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:2226:docs/perf/codex-reviews/f47a44d.md:1040:The framework treats common Nostr-correctness failures (stale replaceable events, lost subscriptions, mis-routed publishes, double-publication, multi-account desync, leaked secrets across FFI, naive cache invalidation, withheld cached data, blocking-on-fetch UI patterns) as **product defects in the framework** rather than as developer mistakes. The public API is designed so that the wrong thing is hard to type.

docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:4707:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:12:- `SessionState` (`subsystems.md` §7.4 lines 107–125) carries `accounts: Vec<Account>` and `active: Option<String>` as plain state fields. A `SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey }` action mutates `active`; the mutation is the only state change.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:4710:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:18:**App writes:** one dispatch: `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey })`. The app's "switch account" UI is a button that fires that dispatch. No log-out / log-in dance, no view-tree rebuild, no manual REQ reissue, no clearing of caches — the framework handles all of it as a single tick of the actor's event loop.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:4712:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:26:3. **Dispatch switch:** `dispatch(AppAction::SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey: bob_pk })`. The test makes no other calls; the harness drains the action ledger and the planner trigger queue.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:4714:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:33:**Milestone owner:** **[PENDING M8]**. M8 is the multi-account session milestone (per `scope-adjustments-2026-05-18.md` ladder). M2 already lands the `Trigger::ActiveAccountChanged` shape (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2 line 109: "M2 establishes the trigger; M8 wires the multi-account state machine"). Test checked in as `#[ignore = "pending M8 multi-account state machine"]`. Sub-paths 4 and 7 are testable as soon as M2 lands (single-account boot fires the trigger once with `from: None, to: Some(active)` per the M2 design); the rest needs M8.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:4715:docs/design/framework-magic/sessions.md:37:The eight sub-paths assert different facets of one observable contract: *after the switch dispatch, every consequence is a derived re-emission, never an imperative reissue.* The kernel-substrate (`kernel-substrate.md` §8) ensures domain-store isolation; the planner (`subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.2) ensures interest re-resolution; the identity machinery (`kernel-substrate.md` §6) ensures signer rebinding. The contract bullet covers all three as one because they are observed together: an app that does `dispatch(SwitchActiveAccount)` and then attempts any operation gets a correctly-rebound system; partial rebinding is a regression.
docs/perf/codex-reviews/de9e7b4.md:4723:docs/design/framework-magic/kind3.md:15:2. Emits an internal planner trigger — proposed name `Trigger::FollowListChanged { account: AccountId, prev_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey>, next_follows: BTreeSet<Pubkey> }` — symmetric to the existing `Trigger::Nip65Arrived` (`docs/design/subscription-compilation/recompilation.md` §4.1).

A `SwitchActiveAccount { pubkey }` action mutates `active`; the mutation is the only state change.
